title: The Effect of Strength Training Methods on Middle-Distance and Long-Distance Runners' Athletic Performance: A Systematic Review with Meta-analysis
notes: Systematically reviewed and meta-analyzed effects of different strength training methods on middle- and long-distance runners' performance including time trials and maximal speed.

title: Effects of Strength Training on Running Economy in Highly Trained Runners: A Systematic Review with Meta-Analysis
notes: Systematically reviewed and meta-analyzed effects of strength training on running economy in highly trained runners, comparing training modalities.

title: VO2max trainability and high intensity interval training in humans: a meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analyzed VO2max improvements from HIIT protocols, quantifying dose-response and effect sizes across training studies.

title: Effects of Strength Training on the Physiological Determinants of Middle- and Long-Distance Running Performance: A Systematic Review
notes: Systematic review examining effects of strength training on VO2max, running economy, body composition, and time trial performance in middle- and long-distance runners.

title: Sprint interval training decreases circulating MicroRNAs important for muscle development
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: Examined effects of sprint interval training on 5km running performance and circulating microRNAs in recreational runners.

title: Explosive-strength training improves 5-km running time by improving running economy and muscle power
notes: Foundational study showing explosive strength training improved 5K time by 3% through enhanced running economy and neuromuscular characteristics, without changing VO2max.

title: Concurrent speed endurance and resistance training improves performance, running economy, and muscle NHE1 in moderately trained runners
notes: Examined how concurrent speed endurance and resistance training improved running performance and economy in moderately trained runners over 8 weeks.

title: Neuromuscular factors determining 5 km running performance and running economy in well-trained athletes
notes: Examined neuromuscular factors (muscle power, stiffness, pre-activation) as determinants of 5km performance and running economy in well-trained distance runners.

title: "Living high-training low": effect of moderate-altitude acclimatization with low-altitude training on performance
notes: The seminal LHTL study. 39 runners randomized to high-low (live 2500m, train 1250m), high-high (live and train 2500m), or low-low control for 4 weeks. High-low group showed significant improvement in 5000m time trial, increased VO2max and red cell mass, while maintaining training velocity. Established the LHTL paradigm that dominates elite endurance training today.

title: "Living high-training low" altitude training improves sea level performance in male and female elite runners
notes: Follow-up to the 1997 landmark study, this time in elite runners (14 men, 8 women). 27 days of living at 2500m and training at 1250m improved 3000m time trial performance by 1.1% with one-third achieving personal bests. Confirmed LHTL works even in already highly-trained athletes, with a 3% VO2max increase and 1 g/dl hemoglobin increase.

title: "Living High-Training Low" for Olympic Medal Performance: What Have We Learned 25 Years After Implementation?
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Comprehensive 25-year retrospective on LHTL implementation for Olympic performance. Covers 10 key lessons: optimal dose (>12h/day at 2000-2500m for 3-4 weeks), individual response variability, iron status requirements, training-load monitoring, wellness tracking, timing relative to competition, natural vs simulated hypoxia, adaptation robustness, broad athlete applicability, and method combinations.

title: Effect of altitude training on the aerobic capacity of athletes: A systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analysis of 17 publications comparing altitude vs sea-level training. Altitude training produced significantly higher VO2max (SMD=0.67, p<0.001) and hemoglobin levels (SMD=0.50, p=0.013). Provides pooled effect sizes confirming altitude training's benefit for aerobic capacity across multiple endurance disciplines.

title: Impact of Altitude Training on Athletes' Aerobic Capacity: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
notes: Most recent meta-analysis (2025) of 13 RCTs with 276 participants aged 18-35. Found altitude training improves aerobic capacity primarily through hematological adaptations and time trial performance, though impact on VO2max per se was minimal. LHTH approach and interventions >3 weeks were most effective. Searched 8 databases following PRISMA guidelines.

title: Optimal type and dose of hypoxic training for improving maximal aerobic capacity in athletes: a systematic review and Bayesian model-based network meta-analysis
notes: Large network meta-analysis of 59 studies (1821 athletes) using Bayesian modeling to compare all major hypoxic training paradigms. Ranked LHTL + low-altitude training as the most effective for VO2max improvement. Differentiates optimal protocols for athletes vs non-athletes (IHIT ranked best for athletes, IHT for non-athletes). Provides the strongest comparative evidence for choosing between altitude training methods.

title: The Effects of Altitude Training on Erythropoietic Response and Hematological Variables in Adult Athletes: A Narrative Review
notes: Comprehensive review of erythropoietic mechanisms underlying altitude training (covering 1997-2017 literature). EPO peaks within 1-3 days of altitude exposure then gradually returns to baseline. Key finding: even significant EPO increases do not guarantee improved hematological variables at sea level. Identifies critical moderators including hypoxic dose, iron stores, training load, genetics, and infection status.

title: Improved race performance in elite middle-distance runners after cumulative altitude exposure
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Studied 7 elite middle-distance runners (VO2max 71.4 ml/min/kg) using a combined approach of simulated LHTL and natural altitude training camps over multiple years. Cumulative altitude exposure improved competitive race performance by 1.9% (90% CL: 1.3-2.5%). Demonstrates that repeated altitude blocks have additive benefits and supports periodized altitude training across a season.

title: Effectiveness of Start to Run, a 6-week training program for novice runners, on increasing health-enhancing physical activity: a controlled study
notes: Evaluated a 6-week Start to Run beginner program in the Netherlands. Participants showed significant increases in vigorous-intensity physical activity and sports activity vs. controls, with 69% still running 4.5 months post-program. Demonstrates that structured beginner running programs produce lasting behavior change.

title: Couch-to-5k or Couch to Ouch to Couch!? Who Takes Part in Beginner Runner Programmes in the UK and Is Non-Completion Linked to Musculoskeletal Injury?
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
notes: First major study examining Couch-to-5K programme completion and injury. Only 27.3% completed the programme; 19% experienced musculoskeletal injuries. Previous injury was the strongest predictor of new injury (OR 7.56). Dropout themes included injury, negative emotions, and programme design issues. Highlights that C25K lacks an evidence base despite popularity.

title: The Start-to-Run Distance and Running-Related Injury Among Obese Novice Runners: A Randomized Trial
journal: International Journal of Sports Physical Therapy
notes: RCT comparing 3 km/week vs 6 km/week start-to-run distance in obese novice runners (BMI 30-35). The lower-distance group had 31.2% fewer injuries and significantly fewer overuse symptoms (cumulative risk difference -47.8%). Critical evidence for conservative volume progression in heavier beginners.

title: Incidence of Running-Related Injuries Per 1000 h of running in Different Types of Runners: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
notes: Meta-analysis of 13 studies quantifying injury incidence per 1000 hours across runner types. Novice runners had the highest injury rate (17.8 per 1000h) compared to recreational runners (7.7 per 1000h). Establishes that beginners face more than double the injury risk of experienced recreational runners, which is essential context for coaching AI to manage training load conservatively.

title: Predictors of Running-Related Injuries Among 930 Novice Runners: A 1-Year Prospective Follow-up Study
notes: Large prospective cohort (930 novice runners, 155,318 km tracked) identifying injury predictors. BMI >30, age 45-65, non-competitive behavior, and previous non-running injuries increased injury risk. BMI <20 was protective. Provides key risk stratification data for personalizing beginner training plans.

title: No effect of a graded training program on the number of running-related injuries in novice runners: a randomized controlled trial
notes: RCT comparing a 13-week graded program (10% rule) vs standard 8-week program in 532 novice runners. No difference in injury rates (~20% in both groups). Challenges the popular 10% weekly increase rule, suggesting that total training volume and individual factors may matter more than progression rate alone for injury prevention in beginners.

title: Meta-Analyses of the Effects of Habitual Running on Indices of Health in Physically Inactive Adults
notes: Meta-analysis of 49 RCTs (22,380 records screened) on health effects of running in previously inactive adults. After ~1 year of training: -3.3 kg body mass, -2.7% body fat, -6.7 bpm resting HR, +7.1 ml/min/kg VO2max, improved lipid profiles. Longer training produced larger benefits. Provides the evidence base for communicating health benefits to beginner runners.

title: Prospective study of beginner running groups: psychological predictors and outcomes of participation
notes: Prospective study of 141 beginner running group members (63% low exercise at entry). 75% of completers achieved 30-minute continuous running. Self-efficacy, adherence, and younger age predicted success. Significant improvements in mental wellbeing, running identity, and social physique anxiety. Shows that psychological factors are critical for beginner program design and retention.

title: The Relationship Between Running Biomechanics and Running Economy: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Observational Studies
notes: Meta-analyzed 51 studies to quantify associations between running biomechanical variables (cadence, vertical oscillation, leg stiffness, joint angles) and running economy.

title: Biomechanical and Musculoskeletal Measurements as Risk Factors for Running-Related Injury in Non-elite Runners: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Prospective Studies
notes: Systematically reviewed 30 prospective studies examining over 100 biomechanical and musculoskeletal measurements as risk factors for running-related injury in non-elite runners.

title: What is the Effect of Changing Running Step Rate on Injury, Performance and Biomechanics? A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
notes: Systematically reviewed 37 studies examining the effects of step rate manipulation on running injury risk, performance, and biomechanics.

title: What are the Benefits and Risks Associated with Changing Foot Strike Pattern During Running? A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Injury, Running Economy, and Biomechanics
notes: Systematically reviewed 53 studies examining benefits and risks of changing foot strike pattern on injury, running economy, and biomechanics.

title: Risk Factors for Injuries in Runners: A Systematic Review of Foot Strike Technique and Its Classification at Impact
notes: Systematically reviewed 13 studies examining foot strike technique classification methods and their relationship to running-related injuries.

title: Biomechanical risk factors for running-related injury differ by sample population: A systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analyzed 13 prospective studies demonstrating that biomechanical risk factors for running injury differ depending on the sample population studied.

title: Effects of step rate manipulation on joint mechanics during running
notes: Characterized the biomechanical effects of step rate modification (preferred +/- 5% and 10%) on hip, knee, and ankle joint mechanics in recreational runners.

title: Running Technique is an Important Component of Running Economy and Performance
notes: Measured 19 biomechanical variables in 97 runners of diverse ability to identify which technique variables most strongly correlate with running economy and performance.

title: Is There an Economical Running Technique? A Review of Modifiable Biomechanical Factors Affecting Running Economy
notes: Reviewed modifiable biomechanical factors (stride length, vertical oscillation, ground contact time, leg stiffness, arm swing) and their effects on running economy.

title: Running economy and lower extremity stiffness in endurance runners: A systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analyzed 13 studies examining the relationship between lower extremity stiffness (vertical stiffness and leg stiffness) and running economy in endurance runners.

title: Running performance in a timed city run and body composition: A cross-sectional study in more than 3000 runners
notes: Cross-sectional study of 3124 runners examining whether body composition (via BIA) predicts running speed better than BMI in a timed city run.

title: Marathon performance in relation to body fat percentage and training indices in recreational male runners
journal: Open Access Journal of Sports Medicine
notes: Developed a predictive equation (r=0.81) for marathon time using body fat percentage, weekly training distance, and mean training pace in 126 recreational male marathoners.

title: Running speed during training and percent body fat predict race time in recreational male marathoners
journal: Open Access Journal of Sports Medicine
notes: Multivariate regression analysis identifying training speed and percent body fat as the two strongest predictors of marathon race time in recreational male runners.

title: Does Muscle Mass Affect Running Times in Male Long-distance Master Runners?
notes: Investigated whether skeletal muscle mass or body fat was associated with race times in 349 male master runners across half-marathon, marathon, and ultra-marathon distances.

title: Relationship Between Body Segment Mass and Running Performance in Well-Trained Endurance Runners
notes: Examined correlations between DXA-measured leg, arm, and trunk segment masses and 5000m personal best times in 37 well-trained endurance runners.

title: Anthropometric profiles and body composition of male runners at different distances
notes: Compared anthropometric profiles and body composition of 68 male academic athletes across sprint, middle-distance, and long-distance running events.

title: Predictive Performance Models in Long-Distance Runners: A Narrative Review
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
notes: Narrative review of 58 studies examining predictive models for 5K through marathon performance, including anthropometric variables and body composition as predictors.

title: Relationship of anthropometric and training characteristics with race performance in endurance and ultra-endurance athletes
notes: Narrative review examining how anthropometric and training characteristics predict race performance across swimming, cycling, running, and triathlon in endurance and ultra-endurance events.

title: Systematic review: Carbohydrate supplementation on exercise performance or capacity of varying durations
notes: Systematically reviewed 61 studies examining carbohydrate supplementation effects on exercise performance across varying durations.

title: A step towards personalized sports nutrition: carbohydrate intake during exercise
notes: Proposed updated carbohydrate intake guidelines stratified by exercise duration and intensity, incorporating multiple transportable carbohydrate research.

title: The use of carbohydrates during exercise as an ergogenic aid
notes: Reviewed evidence for carbohydrate ingestion as ergogenic aid during prolonged exercise, including dose-response and multiple transportable carbohydrate recommendations.

title: Carbohydrates for training and competition
notes: Provided updated guidelines for carbohydrate intake during training and competition, distinguishing high vs low carbohydrate availability strategies.

title: Fructose-Glucose Composite Carbohydrates and Endurance Performance: Critical Review and Future Perspectives
notes: Critically reviewed 14 studies on fructose-glucose composite beverages examining optimal ratios, oxidation rates, and endurance performance effects.

title: High rates of exogenous carbohydrate oxidation from a mixture of glucose and fructose ingested during prolonged cycling exercise
notes: Demonstrated that co-ingesting glucose and fructose at high rates during cycling achieved exogenous carbohydrate oxidation rates of ~1.75 g/min.

title: Glucose and Fructose Hydrogel Enhances Running Performance, Exogenous Carbohydrate Oxidation, and Gastrointestinal Tolerance
journal: Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise
notes: Tested a glucose-fructose hydrogel formulation vs standard solution in trained male runners, finding 2.1% faster time-trial performance with the hydrogel.

title: Gut-training: the impact of two weeks repetitive gut-challenge during exercise on gastrointestinal status, glucose availability, fuel kinetics, and running performance
notes: Investigated a 2-week gut-training protocol in endurance runners, measuring effects on GI tolerance, glucose availability, and running performance.

title: Fuel for the Work Required: A Theoretical Framework for Carbohydrate Periodization and the Glycogen Threshold Hypothesis
notes: Proposed the glycogen threshold hypothesis as a theoretical framework for carbohydrate periodization in endurance training.

title: Performance effects of periodized carbohydrate restriction in endurance trained athletes - a systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition
notes: Conducted a meta-analysis of 9 studies examining whether periodized carbohydrate restriction enhances endurance performance compared to normal carbohydrate availability.

title: Interference of strength development by simultaneously training for strength and endurance
journal: European Journal of Applied Physiology and Occupational Physiology
notes: Foundational study that first demonstrated the interference effect: concurrent strength and endurance training reduced strength development but did not affect VO2max gains.

title: Concurrent training: a meta-analysis examining interference of aerobic and resistance exercises
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: Meta-analysis of 21 studies examining how endurance training modality, frequency, and duration moderate interference with resistance training outcomes including hypertrophy and strength.

title: Interference between concurrent resistance and endurance exercise: molecular bases and the role of individual training variables
notes: Narrative review examining the molecular signaling pathways (mTOR, AMPK) underlying the interference effect and how training variables such as modality, intensity, volume, and sequence modulate it.

title: Concurrent exercise training: do opposites distract?
notes: Review of molecular responses in skeletal muscle during concurrent training, examining evidence for the specificity principle and how divergent training modes may create conflicting adaptive signals.

title: Explosive Training and Heavy Weight Training are Effective for Improving Running Economy in Endurance Athletes: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
notes: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 16 studies examining effects of explosive and heavy weight concurrent strength training on running economy in endurance athletes.

title: Effects of a concurrent strength and endurance training on running performance and running economy in recreational marathon runners
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: RCT examining 8 weeks of concurrent strength and endurance training versus endurance-only training on running performance and economy in recreational marathon runners.

title: Concurrent training in elite male runners: the influence of strength versus muscular endurance training on performance outcomes
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: RCT comparing concurrent maximal strength training versus muscular endurance training versus endurance-only on performance outcomes in elite male runners over 12 weeks.

title: Concurrent strength and endurance training effects on running economy in master endurance runners
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: RCT examining effects of maximal strength training versus resistance training added to endurance training on running economy in master endurance runners.

title: Effects of concurrent training sequence on VO2max and lower limb strength performance: A systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 19 RCTs comparing strength-then-endurance versus endurance-then-strength training sequences on VO2max and lower limb strength outcomes.

title: Concurrent Strength and Endurance Training: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis on the Impact of Sex and Training Status
notes: Meta-analysis of 59 studies examining how sex and training status moderate the interference effect on strength and aerobic adaptations during concurrent training.

title: A Systematic Review of Deep Water Running as an Alternative to Land-Based Running for Improving Physical Performance
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
notes: Systematically reviewed evidence on deep water running as alternative to land-based running for maintaining/improving physical performance during injury or supplementary training.

title: Comparison of Elliptical Training, Stationary Cycling, Treadmill Walking and Overground Walking
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: Compared physiological responses (VO2, HR, RPE) across elliptical, cycling, treadmill walking, and overground walking at matched intensities.

title: Cross-Training and VO2max in High School Cross-Country Runners
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: Examined whether supplementary cross-training (cycling, elliptical) improved VO2max and race performance in high school cross-country runners.

title: Post-Marathon Recovery: Is Active or Passive Recovery Better for Subsequent Performance?
notes: Compared active recovery (cross-training) vs passive recovery following a marathon on subsequent performance markers and muscle damage.

title: Effect of 4 wk of deep water run training on running performance
journal: Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise
notes: Examined whether 4 weeks of deep water running maintained running performance (VO2max, economy) compared to land-based training.

title: Influence of water run training on the maintenance of aerobic performance
journal: Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise
notes: Examined whether deep water running maintained aerobic performance (VO2max, treadmill time) over 6 weeks in trained runners compared to land-based training.

title: Energy Expenditure during Treadmill Walking, Elliptical and Stair Stepper Exercise with Matched Oxygen Consumption
journal: International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism
notes: Compared energy expenditure across treadmill, elliptical, and stair stepper at matched oxygen consumption to determine relative exercise equivalency.

title: Effects of cross-training. Transfer of training effects on VO2max between cycling, running and swimming
notes: Reviewed evidence on transfer of training effects between cycling, running, and swimming, examining specificity principle and practical cross-training applications.

title: Effects of Short- and Long-Term Detraining on Maximal Oxygen Uptake in Athletes: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
notes: Meta-analysis of 21 studies quantifying VO2max decline during short-term vs long-term training cessation across multiple sports.

title: Cardiorespiratory and metabolic consequences of detraining in endurance athletes
notes: Systematic review of cardiorespiratory, metabolic, and muscular detraining consequences in endurance athletes with minimum fitness thresholds.

title: Time course of loss of adaptations after stopping prolonged intense endurance training
notes: Tracked physiological decline at 12, 21, 56, and 84 days after training cessation in endurance-trained subjects, establishing the detraining timeline.

title: Two weeks of detraining reduces cardiopulmonary function and muscular fitness in endurance athletes
notes: Measured cardiopulmonary and muscular fitness changes after 14 days of complete training cessation in young male endurance athletes.

title: Reduced training frequencies and maintenance of increased aerobic power
notes: Demonstrated that VO2max gains from 10 weeks of training were maintained for 15 weeks with only 2-4 sessions per week if intensity was preserved.

title: Reduced training intensities and loss of aerobic power, endurance, and cardiac growth
notes: Showed that reducing training intensity by one-third or two-thirds (with same frequency/duration) caused progressive loss of aerobic power and cardiac adaptations.

title: Maintaining Physical Performance: The Minimal Dose of Exercise Needed to Preserve Endurance and Strength Over Time
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: Reviewed minimum exercise dose to maintain endurance and strength, concluding intensity is the key variable even with large reductions in frequency and volume.

title: Effect of 12 weeks of detraining and retraining on the cardiorespiratory fitness in a competitive master athlete: a case study
notes: Tracked physiological decline and recovery in a 53-year-old master triathlete through 12 weeks of detraining and 12 weeks of retraining with molecular analysis.

title: Influence of exercise intensity and duration on biochemical adaptations in skeletal muscle
journal: Journal of Applied Physiology: Respiratory, Environmental and Exercise Physiology
notes: Examined the interaction of exercise intensity and daily duration on mitochondrial enzyme adaptations across fiber types using 19 different treadmill protocols in rats.

title: Effects of low intensity aerobic training on skeletal muscle capillary and blood lipoprotein profiles
notes: Examined effects of low-intensity aerobic training on skeletal muscle capillarization and lipoprotein profiles in 11 non-obese men.

title: Optimizing fat oxidation through exercise and diet
notes: Reviewed how exercise intensity affects fat oxidation rates, identifying maximal fat oxidation at 59-64% VO2max in trained individuals.

title: Blood lactate clearance during active recovery after an intense running bout depends on the intensity of the active recovery
notes: Examined blood lactate clearance at different active recovery intensities after intense running in 10 males, finding intensity-dependent clearance.

title: Effects of one year aerobic endurance training on resting metabolic rate and exercise fat oxidation in previously untrained men and women. Metabolic endurance training adaptations
notes: Examined metabolic adaptations including resting metabolic rate and fat oxidation changes after one year of aerobic endurance training in 17 previously untrained participants.

title: Active vs. passive recovery during an aerobic interval training session in well-trained runners
notes: Compared active versus passive recovery during interval training in 11 well-trained distance runners examining physiological and performance responses.

title: Effects of Exercise Training on Mitochondrial and Capillary Growth in Human Skeletal Muscle: A Systematic Review and Meta-Regression
notes: Systematic review and meta-regression of 353 articles (5,973 participants) examining how exercise training variables affect mitochondrial and capillary growth in human skeletal muscle.

title: Much Ado About Zone 2: A Narrative Review Assessing the Efficacy of Zone 2 Training for Improving Mitochondrial Capacity and Cardiorespiratory Fitness in the General Population
notes: Critically reviewed the popular endorsement of zone 2 training for mitochondrial capacity and cardiorespiratory fitness in the general population.

title: The Effects of Menstrual Cycle Phase on Exercise Performance in Eumenorrheic Women: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
notes: Landmark meta-analysis finding exercise performance may be trivially reduced during early follicular phase compared to all other phases, but effect size is trivial with large between-study variation. Recommends individualized rather than generalized approach to menstrual cycle-based training. Included both endurance and strength outcomes.

title: The Effects of Menstrual Cycle Phase on Elite Athlete Performance: A Critical and Systematic Review
notes: Systematic review focused specifically on elite athletes finding variable associations between menstrual cycle phase and performance outcomes including endurance and power. Female athletes perceive performance to be worse during early follicular and late luteal phases, but objective studies do not show clear, consistent effects.

title: The Effects of Oral Contraceptives on Exercise Performance in Women: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
notes: Companion meta-analysis to McNulty 2020, examining oral contraceptive pill (OCP) effects on exercise performance. Found OCP use might result in slightly inferior performance compared to naturally menstruating women, but group-level effects are most likely trivial. Current evidence does not warrant general guidance against OCP use for athletes.

title: Effects of Follicular and Luteal Phase-Based Menstrual Cycle Resistance Training on Muscle Strength and Mass
notes: Critical review of menstrual cycle phase-based periodization for resistance training. Finds follicular phase-based resistance training may be superior to luteal phase-based training for building strength and muscle mass, though evidence base is limited. Important for female runners incorporating strength work into training.

title: Physical Activity and Exercise During Pregnancy and the Postpartum Period: ACOG Committee Opinion, Number 804
notes: Definitive ACOG clinical guidance on exercise during pregnancy and postpartum. Recommends women who were physically active before pregnancy can continue vigorous-intensity activity. States physical activity in pregnancy is safe and desirable in absence of obstetric complications, with benefits including reduced gestational diabetes and faster postpartum recovery.

title: Physical activity during pregnancy: a systematic review for the assessment of current evidence with future recommendations
journal: BMC Sports Science, Medicine and Rehabilitation
notes: Systematic review of RCTs on exercise during pregnancy. Recommends moderate intensity activities throughout pregnancy, with running and yoga suitable in first trimester, transitioning to aquatic exercise and walking as pregnancy progresses. Exercise 35-90 min 3-4 times/week not associated with increased preterm birth risk.

title: Infographic. Guidance for medical, health and fitness professionals to support women in returning to running postnatally
notes: The peer-reviewed BJSM publication of the landmark 2019 Goom/Donnelly/Brockwell postnatal running guidelines. Recommends no running before 12 weeks postpartum, pelvic floor physiotherapy assessment before returning, and criteria-based progression including strength screening, impact drills, and symptom monitoring. First guidelines specifically for postnatal runners.

title: Clinical and exercise professional opinion on designing a postpartum return-to-running training programme: an international Delphi study and consensus statement
notes: International Delphi consensus on designing postpartum return-to-running programmes. Key recommendations include individualised exercise prescription, gradual activity increases, walk-run protocols, and targeted muscle strengthening. Training should be modified based on musculoskeletal/pelvic symptoms, sleep, mental health, lactation, and energy availability.

title: Navigating the 'new normal': what guidelines exist for postpartum return to physical activity and sport? A scoping review
notes: Scoping review identifying a lack of consistent, evidence-based guidelines for postpartum return to activity/sport. Highlights multiple evidence gaps requiring additional research to inform patient- and activity-specific guidelines. Important for understanding the current state of postpartum exercise science.

title: Iron deficiency, supplementation, and sports performance in female athletes: A systematic review
journal: Journal of Sport and Health Science
notes: Systematic review finding up to 60% of female athletes experience iron deficiency, with endurance performance negatively affected by 3-4%. Iron supplementation (~100mg elemental iron/day) improved performance by 2-20% in iron-deficient athletes. Recommends consultation with sports medicine practitioners for supplementation protocols.

title: Iron considerations for the athlete: a narrative review
notes: Comprehensive narrative review covering iron's role in athletic performance, with prevalence of iron deficiency at 15-35% in female athletes vs 5-11% in males. Examines impact of sex hormones, diet, training, altitude, and exercise-induced hepcidin elevation on iron metabolism. Provides practical recommendations for managing iron status in endurance athletes.

title: Sex Differences in Endurance Running
notes: Comprehensive review of sex differences in endurance running covering the 10-12% performance gap. Males have higher hemoglobin, larger hearts/lungs, and greater VO2max. Females show advantages in type I fibre proportion, fat oxidation, and pacing strategy. Sex-specific biomechanical differences exist including greater non-sagittal hip/knee motion in females. Important for understanding why coaching approaches may need sex-specific adjustments.

title: A Meta-Analysis of the Effects of Foam Rolling on Performance and Recovery
notes: Meta-analysis examining pre-exercise and post-exercise foam rolling effects on sprint, jump, strength performance, flexibility, and muscle pain.

title: A systematic review and meta-analysis of the effects of foam rolling on range of motion, recovery and markers of athletic performance
journal: Journal of Bodywork and Movement Therapies
notes: Systematic review and meta-analysis of foam rolling effects on ROM, recovery markers, and athletic performance across multiple sports.

title: The Effects of Self-Myofascial Release Using a Foam Roll or Roller Massager on Joint Range of Motion, Muscle Recovery, and Performance: A Systematic Review
journal: International Journal of Sports Physical Therapy
notes: Systematic review of 14 studies examining self-myofascial release via foam roll or roller massager on ROM, muscle recovery, and performance.

title: Foam Rolling for Delayed-Onset Muscle Soreness and Recovery of Dynamic Performance Measures
notes: RCT examining foam rolling effects on DOMS, pressure-pain threshold, sprint speed, power, and dynamic strength-endurance after muscle-damaging exercise.

title: The Influence of Self-Myofascial Release on Muscle Flexibility in Long-Distance Runners
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
notes: RCT examining acute effects of self-myofascial release on muscle flexibility in 62 recreational long-distance runners.

title: Acute effects of muscle stretching on physical performance, range of motion, and injury incidence in healthy active individuals: a systematic review
notes: Systematic review comparing acute effects of static, dynamic, and PNF stretching on performance, ROM, and injury incidence in active individuals.

title: Acute and Chronic Effects of Stretching on Running Economy: A Systematic Review with Meta-Analysis
notes: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 15 acute and 2 chronic studies examining effects of static, dynamic, and PNF stretching on running economy.

title: The association between flexibility and running economy in sub-elite male distance runners
notes: Cross-sectional study examining the association between lower-body flexibility measures and running economy in 19 sub-elite male distance runners.

title: Familial aggregation of VO(2max) response to exercise training: results from the HERITAGE Family Study
notes: Examined familial aggregation and heritability of VO2max training response in 98 two-generation families undergoing 20 weeks of standardized cycle ergometer training.

title: Genomic predictors of the maximal O2 uptake response to standardized exercise training programs
notes: Conducted a GWAS on HERITAGE Family Study participants identifying 21 SNPs that accounted for 49% of variance in VO2max trainability, with replication in STRRIDE and DREW cohorts.

title: The association of sport performance with ACE and ACTN3 genetic polymorphisms: a systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analysis of ACE and ACTN3 polymorphism studies examining associations between genotype and endurance vs power athletic performance.

title: Genes to predict VO2max trainability: a systematic review
notes: Systematic review identifying 97 genes associated with VO2max trainability across published intervention studies, noting limited replication and predominantly Caucasian samples.

title: Refuting the myth of non-response to exercise training: 'non-responders' do respond to higher dose of training
notes: RCT assigning 78 healthy adults to five different weekly exercise doses showing that non-response to endurance training is dose-dependent and disappears at higher training volumes.

title: Do Non-Responders to Exercise Exist-and If So, What Should We Do About Them?
notes: Narrative review examining whether true non-responders to exercise exist, concluding that global non-response is unlikely and alternative training modalities can rescue apparent non-responders.

title: Can Genetic Testing Identify Talent for Sport?
notes: Review examining whether genetic testing can predict future elite athletic performance, discussing current limitations and potential utility for guiding training and nutrition.

title: Genes and Elite Marathon Running Performance: A Systematic Review
journal: Journal of Sports Science and Medicine
notes: Systematic review of 14 studies identifying 160 polymorphisms in 27 genes among marathon runners, finding 16 SNPs in 14 genes potentially associated with marathon performance.

title: Biological and methodological factors affecting VO2max response variability to endurance training and the influence of exercise intensity prescription
notes: Review examining biological factors (genetics, age, sex, baseline fitness) and methodological factors (training type, volume, intensity prescription) affecting VO2max response variability to endurance training.

title: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of the Association Between ACTN3 R577X Genotypes and Performance in Endurance Versus Power Athletes and Non-athletes
notes: Meta-analysis of 25 studies across 13 countries examining ACTN3 R577X genotype distribution in power vs endurance athletes vs non-athletes.

title: Predictive Variables of Half-Marathon Performance for Male Runners
journal: Journal of Sports Science and Medicine
notes: Established and validated predictive equations for HM performance using 78 male runners; physiological variables predicted 94.9% of variance, training variables 90.3%, anthropometric 93.7%, and combined model 96.2%.

title: Predictors of half-marathon performance in male recreational athletes
notes: Developed prediction formula for HM time in 134 recreational runners: Race time = 80.056 + 2.498×BMI - 0.594×VO2max - 0.191×weekly training distance (R²=0.63).

title: Participation and performance characteristics in half-marathon run: a brief narrative review
journal: Journal of Muscle Research and Cell Motility
notes: Reviewed participation trends, performance characteristics, sex and age differences in half-marathon running, providing context for training prescription and performance expectations.

title: Cooper Test Provides Better Half-Marathon Performance Prediction in Recreational Runners Than Laboratory Tests
notes: Compared field (Cooper test) vs laboratory (treadmill VO2max, vVO2max) for predicting HM time in recreational runners; Cooper test distance was the best single predictor of HM performance.

title: Predictor variables for a half marathon race time in recreational male runners
journal: Open Access Journal of Sports Medicine
notes: Identified training and anthropometric predictor variables for HM race time in 84 recreational male runners, finding speed in training and body fat most predictive.

title: Effect of performance level on pacing strategy during a 10-km running race
notes: Examined pacing strategy differences across performance levels in 10K races; high-performance runners adopted a fast-start strategy while low-performance runners used even pacing. Peak treadmill velocity, LT, and RE correlated with pacing choices.

title: The pacing differences in performance levels of marathon and half-marathon runners
notes: Analyzed pacing across 208,760 HM and marathon finishers (2006-2018); better runners showed more even pacing, HM runners paced more evenly than marathoners across all performance groups.

title: Some physiological demands of a half-marathon race on recreational runners
notes: Measured VO2, blood lactate, and energy expenditure during a half-marathon in recreational runners to quantify the physiological demands and determine that average race intensity was 79% VO2max.

title: A comparison of the physiological demands imposed by competing in a half-marathon vs. a marathon
journal: Journal of Sports Medicine and Physical Fitness
notes: Compared physiological responses (muscle fatigue, fiber damage, pain, fluid/electrolyte balance) between matched pairs of half-marathoners and marathoners running at similar speeds to distinguish the demands of each distance.

title: Energy system contribution in a maximal incremental test: correlations with pacing and overall performance in a 10-km running trial
journal: Brazilian Journal of Medical and Biological Research
notes: Assessed whether aerobic and glycolytic energy system contributions measured during an incremental treadmill test correlated with pacing and overall performance during an outdoor 10-km race.

title: Impact of training intensity distribution on performance in endurance athletes
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: Randomly assigned 12 sub-elite runners to two 5-month programs differing in low- vs moderate-intensity volume distribution and compared effects on 10.4-km cross-country performance.

title: Does polarized training improve performance in recreational runners?
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Randomly assigned 30 recreational runners to polarized or between-thresholds training over 10 weeks and compared changes in 10-km race performance and physiological markers.

title: What is best practice for training intensity and duration distribution in endurance athletes?
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Synthesized research on training intensity distribution patterns across competitive endurance sports, establishing the ~80/20 low-to-high intensity framework as typical best practice for trained athletes.

title: Age-predicted maximal heart rate revisited
journal: Journal of the American College of Cardiology
notes: Meta-analysis of 351 studies (18,712 subjects) plus laboratory validation (n=514). Derived revised HRmax formula 208 - 0.7 x age (r = -0.90). Relationship consistent across genders and fitness levels. Commonly cited 220-age formula underestimates HRmax in older adults. Foundational paper for modern HRmax estimation.

title: Age-predicted maximal heart rate in healthy subjects: The HUNT fitness study
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports
notes: Large population study (3,320 healthy men and women) deriving HRmax = 211 - 0.64 x age (SEE = 10.8 bpm). No significant interactions between HRmax and gender, physical activity level, BMI, or aerobic fitness. Found HRmax in healthy older subjects and women was higher than previously reported.

title: Accuracy of Commonly Used Age-Predicted Maximal Heart Rate Equations
notes: Validated nine age-predicted HRmax equations against measured GXT HRmax in 99 participants (ages 17-67). All nine equations showed poor agreement with measured HRmax (wide limits of agreement). Fox equation (220-age) had least proportional bias. Recommends GXT-measured HRmax when applicable.

title: Age-Predicted Maximal Heart Rate in Recreational Marathon Runners: A Cross-Sectional Study on Fox's and Tanaka's Equations
notes: Running-specific validation of Fox (220-age) and Tanaka (208-0.7xage) formulas in 180 recreational marathon runners (mean age 43.2, mean VO2max 46.8 mL/min/kg). Fox and Tanaka overestimated HRmax by ~5 bpm in women. Fox underestimated by ~3 bpm in men. Tanaka closely aligned with measured values in men. Demonstrates need for running-specific HRmax testing.

title: Heart rate reserve is equivalent to %VO2 reserve, not to %VO2max
notes: Foundational paper establishing that %HRR (Karvonen method) is equivalent to %VO2 reserve, NOT %VO2max as previously assumed. Tested in 63 adults on cycle ergometer. Discrepancy inversely related to fitness level. Changed ACSM exercise prescription guidelines. Replicated on treadmill by same group in 1998 (PMID 9502363, n=50).

title: Validity of the heart rate deflection point as a predictor of lactate threshold during running
notes: Tested Conconi HR deflection point method as non-invasive predictor of lactate threshold in 8 trained male runners using track Conconi test, continuous treadmill test, and lactate protocol. HR deflection point overestimated LT when continuous treadmill protocol used. Concluded HR deflection is not a reliable predictor of lactate threshold during running.

title: Heart Rate Variability-Derived Thresholds for Exercise Intensity Prescription in Endurance Sports: A Systematic Review of Interrelations and Agreement with Different Ventilatory and Blood Lactate Thresholds
notes: Systematic review of 27 studies (461 participants) comparing HRV-derived thresholds with ventilatory and blood lactate thresholds for exercise intensity zone demarcation. HRVTlow shows promise as non-invasive proxy for first ventilatory threshold (zone 1/2 boundary). High overall correlation but heterogeneous agreement at individual level. Identified need for female participants and standardised methods.

title: Polarized training has greater impact on key endurance variables than threshold, high intensity, or high volume training
notes: RCT comparing four HR-zone-based training approaches (polarized, threshold, HIIT, high volume) over 9 weeks in 48 well-trained endurance athletes (VO2peak 62.6 mL/min/kg). Polarized training produced greatest VO2peak increase (+6.8 mL/min/kg, +11.7%) and TTE improvement (+17.4%). Demonstrates superiority of polarized HR zone distribution for trained athletes. Training zones defined by individual HR at ventilatory thresholds.

title: Impact of weather on marathon-running performance
notes: Landmark study quantifying the impact of WBGT on marathon performance. Found progressive slowing as WBGT increases from 5 to 25C, with slower runners more affected than faster runners. Established that performance degradation is proportional to thermal stress and varies by ability level. Essential reference for pace adjustment calculators in hot conditions.

title: Effects of Weather Parameters on Endurance Running Performance: Discipline-specific Analysis of 1258 Races
notes: Largest analysis of weather on running performance: 1258 races (1936-2019) across 42 countries covering marathon, 50km racewalk, 20km racewalk, 10000m, 5000m, and steeplechase. Found optimal WBGT of 7.5-15C (air temp 10-17.5C), with 0.3-0.4% performance decline per degree outside optimum. WBGT had better predictive power than air temperature alone (R2=0.11-0.47 vs 0.04-0.34). Provides discipline-specific adjustment factors.

title: Exercise under heat stress: thermoregulation, hydration, performance implications, and mitigation strategies
notes: The definitive modern comprehensive review (107 pages) on exercise in heat. Covers thermoregulation mechanisms, cardiovascular strain, CNS fatigue, dehydration effects, heat acclimation protocols, cooling strategies, and hydration guidelines. Essential single reference for understanding the full scope of heat-exercise interactions. Supersedes older reviews with updated evidence through 2020.

title: Consensus recommendations on training and competing in the heat
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports
notes: Expert consensus from 17 leading researchers providing actionable recommendations for athletes training/competing in heat. Key guidance: 1-2 weeks heat acclimatization with repeated exercise-heat exposures, start competition euhydrated, minimize dehydration, use pre-cooling and per-cooling strategies. The go-to practical reference for coaches preparing athletes for hot-weather events.

title: The Effects of Heat Adaptation on Physiology, Perception and Exercise Performance in the Heat: A Meta-Analysis
notes: Meta-analysis quantifying heat adaptation benefits: improved exercise performance/capacity (g=0.7), reduced resting core temperature (g=-0.6), reduced heart rate (g=-0.5). Found medium-term protocols (8-14 days) most common; long-term HA showed highest effect sizes. Provides pooled evidence that heat adaptation significantly and meaningfully improves performance in the heat, with specific effect sizes coaches can use to justify the investment.

title: Heat Acclimation Decay and Re-Induction: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
notes: Critical for heat acclimation programming: quantified that HR and core temperature adaptations decay at ~2.5% per day without heat exposure. After 2 weeks without heat exposure, end-exercise HR decayed ~35%, sweat rate ~30%, and core temp ~6%. Full acclimation lost within a month. Re-induction possible with 2-4 days of exercise-heat training. Essential for timing heat acclimation blocks relative to target races.

title: Heat acclimation improves exercise performance
notes: Landmark RCT showing 10 days of heat acclimation (40C) improved 1-hour time trial performance by 5-8% in BOTH hot AND cool (13C) conditions vs control group. Also increased VO2max, lactate threshold, and cardiac output. Demonstrated that heat acclimation is not just for hot races — it has ergogenic effects even in temperate conditions, making it a legitimate training tool year-round. N=20 (12 heat, 8 control).

title: Cold Stress Effects on Exposure Tolerance and Exercise Performance
notes: The most comprehensive review on cold and exercise performance. Covers cold shock responses, hypothermia physiology, and performance effects. Found aerobic performance is degraded in cold environments through decreased muscle temperature, impaired cardiovascular function, increased carbohydrate reliance, and higher lactate production. Skin cooling alone reduces endurance by ~30%, with core cooling adding another 30-40%. Essential for understanding why pacing needs adjustment in cold as well as heat.

title: Heart Rate Variability-Guided Training for Enhancing Cardiac-Vagal Modulation, Aerobic Fitness, and Endurance Performance: A Methodological Systematic Review with Meta-Analysis
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
notes: Systematically reviewed and meta-analyzed studies comparing HRV-guided versus predefined training on cardiac-vagal modulation, aerobic fitness, and endurance performance.

title: HRV-Based Training for Improving VO2max in Endurance Athletes. A Systematic Review with Meta-Analysis
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
notes: Meta-analyzed RCTs comparing HRV-guided training to predefined training for VO2max improvement in endurance athletes.

title: Monitoring Athletic Training Status Through Autonomic Heart Rate Regulation: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
notes: Systematically reviewed and meta-analyzed 27 studies examining how autonomic HR regulation indices (including HRV) respond to changes in training status in athletes.

title: Endurance training guided individually by daily heart rate variability measurements
notes: Randomized 26 moderately fit males into HRV-guided, predefined, and control groups to compare daily HRV-based training prescription versus traditional periodization over 4 weeks.

title: Individual Endurance Training Prescription with Heart Rate Variability
notes: Randomized 40 recreational endurance runners into HRV-guided and predefined training groups to examine whether daily HRV can individualize the timing of vigorous training sessions.

title: Heart rate variability-guided training in professional runners: Effects on performance and vagal modulation
notes: Conducted an 8-week cluster-RCT comparing HRV-guided versus traditional training in 12 professional endurance runners on performance and vagal modulation outcomes.

title: Comparison of Heart-Rate-Variability Recording With Smartphone Photoplethysmography, Polar H7 Chest Strap, and Electrocardiography
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Validated smartphone PPG (HRV4Training) and chest strap rMSSD measurements against ECG in 29 subjects during guided and normal breathing conditions.

title: Practices and Applications of Heart Rate Variability Monitoring in Endurance Athletes
notes: Reviewed current practices and practical applications of HRV monitoring in endurance athletes, covering analysis techniques, consumer tools, training prescription, and performance prediction.

title: Effect of exercise-induced dehydration on time-trial exercise performance: a meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analysis quantifying the effect of exercise-induced dehydration on self-paced time-trial endurance performance across multiple studies.

title: Impact of dehydration on perceived exertion during endurance exercise: A systematic review with meta-analysis
notes: Systematic review with meta-analysis examining whether exercise-induced dehydration meaningfully increases ratings of perceived exertion during endurance exercise.

title: Does Hypohydration Really Impair Endurance Performance? Methodological Considerations for Interpreting Hydration Research
notes: Methodological review examining how blinding failures, dehydration protocols, and study designs may have confounded prior research on dehydration and endurance performance.

title: Hyponatremia among runners in the Boston Marathon
notes: Prospective cohort study estimating hyponatremia incidence and identifying risk factors among 2002 Boston Marathon runners.

title: Statement of the Third International Exercise-Associated Hyponatremia Consensus Development Conference, Carlsbad, California, 2015
notes: International expert consensus statement on the diagnosis, prevention, and treatment of exercise-associated hyponatremia, replacing the second consensus statement.

title: Drinking Strategies: Planned Drinking Versus Drinking to Thirst
notes: Narrative review comparing planned/scheduled fluid intake versus ad libitum thirst-driven drinking during exercise of varying duration and intensity.

title: Sweating Rate and Sweat Sodium Concentration in Athletes: A Review of Methodology and Intra/Interindividual Variability
notes: Methodological review examining sources of intra- and inter-individual variability in sweating rate and sweat sodium concentration during exercise.

title: Prospective Observational Study of Weight-based Assessment of Sodium Supplements on Ultramarathon Performance (WASSUP)
notes: Prospective observational study examining whether ad libitum sodium supplementation affects pace, finish time, or sodium balance during multi-stage ultramarathons.

title: Effects of therapeutic interventions on pain due to plantar fasciitis: A systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: Largest meta-analysis of PF treatment (236 RCTs, 15401 patients); found ESWT was the only intervention effective at medium and long-term follow-up.

title: Risk Factors for Plantar Fasciitis in Physically Active Individuals: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analysis of 16 studies identifying three significant risk factors for PF in active populations: increased plantarflexion ROM, elevated BMI, and increased body mass.

title: High-load strength training improves outcome in patients with plantar fasciitis: A randomized controlled trial with 12-month follow-up
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine and Science in Sports
notes: Landmark RCT establishing high-load strength training protocol (unilateral heel raises with towel under toes) for PF; strength group had FFI 29 points lower than stretch group at 3 months.

title: Management of plantar heel pain: a best practice guide informed by a systematic review, expert clinical reasoning and patient values
notes: Established stepped-care treatment pathway from 51 RCTs: core treatment (taping, stretching, education), add shockwave at 4 weeks, custom orthoses at 12 weeks. Guides return-to-run decision-making.

title: Biomechanical and anatomic factors associated with a history of plantar fasciitis in female runners
notes: Found PF history in female runners associated with greater vertical GRF loading rates and lower medial longitudinal arch height; no difference in rearfoot motion, challenging the overpronation hypothesis.

title: Plantar fasciitis injury status influences foot mechanics during running
notes: Demonstrated active PF alters forefoot and midfoot mechanics during running compared to resolved cases; foot mechanics do not simply return to normal after PF resolution.

title: A systematic review of running-related musculoskeletal injuries in runners
journal: Journal of Sport and Health Science
notes: Systematic review of running-related musculoskeletal injury incidence and prevalence by anatomical location, reporting ankle-foot injuries at ~15% incidence and plantar fasciitis as a top specific diagnosis.

title: The effectiveness of non-surgical interventions for common plantar digital compressive neuropathy (Morton's neuroma): a systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: Journal of Foot and Ankle Research
notes: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 25 studies (7 RCTs, 18 case series) evaluating eight non-surgical interventions for Morton's neuroma, finding strongest evidence for corticosteroid injections and manipulation/mobilisation.

title: Return to sport following navicular stress fracture: a systematic review and meta-analysis of three hundred and fifteen fractures
notes: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 315 navicular stress fractures in athletes comparing operative (98.7% return to sport) versus conservative management outcomes and return-to-sport timelines.

title: Effectiveness of foot orthoses and shock-absorbing insoles for the prevention of injury: a systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analysis of 18 trials finding foot orthoses effective for preventing overall injuries (RR 0.72) and stress fractures (RR 0.59) but not soft-tissue injuries; shock-absorbing insoles showed no benefit.

title: Stage I and II Posterior Tibial Tendon Dysfunction: Return to Running?
notes: Clinical review examining early-stage posterior tibial tendon dysfunction in runners, addressing diagnosis, conservative management with orthotics and physical therapy, and criteria for return to running versus surgical reconstruction.

title: Managing Hallux Rigidus in the Elite Athlete
notes: Clinical review of hallux rigidus management in elite athletes covering conservative strategies (NSAIDs, injections, shoe modifications, orthotics) and surgical options including cheilectomy for predictable return to sport.

title: Runners with patellofemoral pain have altered biomechanics which targeted interventions can modify: A systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: Systematically reviewed biomechanical differences in runners with PFP vs controls, identifying modifiable factors including hip adduction, contralateral pelvic drop, and rearfoot eversion.

title: Infographic. Running myths: common beliefs about running and knee osteoarthritis and patellofemoral pain
notes: Systematic review addressing common myths about running and knee health, synthesizing evidence on running, knee OA risk, and patellofemoral pain.

title: Risk factors for patellofemoral pain: a systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analyzed prospective studies identifying risk factors for developing patellofemoral pain, including quadriceps weakness and increased knee valgus.

title: A Contemporary Approach to Patellofemoral Pain in Runners
notes: Contemporary evidence-based approach to managing PFP in runners, emphasizing load management, activity modification, and graduated return to running.

title: Conservative Treatment of Iliotibial Band Syndrome: A Systematic Review
journal: Frontiers in Sports and Active Living
notes: Systematically reviewed conservative treatment approaches for ITBS including exercise therapy, manual therapy, and combined interventions.

title: Iliotibial band syndrome in runners: a systematic review
notes: Comprehensive systematic review of ITBS in runners covering etiology, risk factors, prevention, and treatment evidence.

title: Iliotibial band syndrome rehabilitation in female runners: a pilot randomized study
journal: Journal of Orthopaedic Surgery and Research
notes: Pilot RCT comparing hip strengthening plus conventional treatment vs conventional treatment alone for ITBS in female runners.

title: Therapeutic targets for iliotibial band syndrome: A scoping review
notes: Scoping review identifying therapeutic targets for ITBS including hip abductor strengthening, ITB flexibility, and running gait modification.

title: Which treatment is most effective for patients with Achilles tendinopathy? A living systematic review with network meta-analysis of 29 randomised controlled trials
notes: Living network meta-analysis of 29 RCTs comparing treatments for Achilles tendinopathy, finding exercise-based treatments superior to wait-and-see.

title: Exercise-Based Treatment for Achilles Tendinopathy: A Systematic Review of the Characteristics of the Therapeutic Exercise Programs
notes: Systematically reviewed exercise program characteristics (type, dosage, progression) for Achilles tendinopathy treatment across RCTs.

title: Heavy Slow Resistance Versus Eccentric Training as Treatment for Achilles Tendinopathy: A Randomized Controlled Trial
notes: RCT comparing heavy slow resistance (HSR) vs Alfredson eccentric protocol for midportion Achilles tendinopathy. Both effective at 12 weeks; HSR showed higher patient satisfaction.

title: Current Clinical Concepts: Conservative Management of Achilles Tendinopathy
notes: Evidence-based clinical concepts review for conservative Achilles tendinopathy management including loading progression, pain monitoring, and return-to-sport criteria.

title: Medial Tibial Stress Syndrome in Active Individuals: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis of Risk Factors
notes: Meta-analyzed prospective studies to identify risk factors for MTSS including prior history, female sex, increased BMI, navicular drop, and hip ROM.

title: Risk factors associated with medial tibial stress syndrome in runners: a systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: Open Access Journal of Sports Medicine
notes: Runner-specific meta-analysis of MTSS risk factors, identifying prior MTSS history, female sex, higher BMI, navicular drop, and ankle plantarflexion ROM as significant predictors.

title: Calf muscle strain injuries in sport: a systematic review of risk factors for injury
notes: Systematic review of risk factors for calf muscle strain injuries in sport, identifying older age, previous injury, and reduced ankle dorsiflexion as key factors.

title: A systematic review and meta-analysis of the association between lower leg muscle morphology and medial tibial stress syndrome
journal: Journal of Foot and Ankle Research
notes: Meta-analyzed associations between lower leg muscle morphology (cross-sectional area, lean mass) and MTSS risk, finding smaller calf muscle size associated with MTSS.

title: Incidence and determinants of lower extremity running injuries in long distance runners: a systematic review
notes: Systematically reviewed 17 published reports to determine incidence rates and identify determinants of lower extremity running injuries in long distance runners.

title: The effectiveness of exercise interventions to prevent sports injuries: a systematic review and meta-analysis of randomised controlled trials
notes: Meta-analysis of 25 RCTs determining whether exercise interventions (strength training, stretching, proprioception, multicomponent) reduce acute and overuse sports injuries. Strength training reduced injuries by ~70%.

title: Gait Retraining for the Reduction of Injury Occurrence in Novice Distance Runners: 1-Year Follow-up of a Randomized Controlled Trial
notes: 1-year follow-up RCT evaluating a 2-week real-time visual feedback gait retraining program on injury occurrence in 320 novice distance runners compared to treadmill control.

title: Is There Evidence for an Association Between Changes in Training Load and Running-Related Injuries? A Systematic Review
journal: International Journal of Sports Physical Therapy
notes: Systematically reviewed evidence on associations between changes in training load parameters and risk of running-related injuries.

title: Strength training as superior, dose-dependent and safe prevention of acute and overuse sports injuries: a systematic review, qualitative analysis and meta-analysis
notes: Dose-response meta-analysis of 6 RCTs quantifying the relationship between strength training volume and acute/overuse injury prevention, establishing a dose-response curve for protective effects.

title: Biomechanical Risk Factors Associated with Running-Related Injuries: A Systematic Review
notes: Systematically reviewed 16 prospective studies evaluating biomechanical variables as risk factors for running-related injuries in previously uninjured runners.

title: Risk factors for running-related injuries: An umbrella systematic review
journal: Journal of Sport and Health Science
notes: Umbrella systematic review synthesizing 13 prior systematic reviews covering 148 primary studies and 207 outcomes to consolidate and classify the full range of risk factors for running-related injuries.

title: Risk factors associated with lower extremity stress fractures in runners: a systematic review with meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analyzed risk factors for lower extremity stress fractures in runners, identifying female sex, menstrual irregularity, calf girth, and training history as significant factors.

title: Bone stress injuries in runners: an international Delphi consensus and meta-analysis
notes: International Delphi consensus with meta-analysis on BSI diagnosis, classification, and return-to-sport criteria for runners.

title: Running biomechanics associated with tibial stress fracture in runners: A systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: Journal of Sport and Health Science
notes: Meta-analyzed biomechanical risk factors for tibial stress fractures in runners including loading rate, tibial acceleration, and rearfoot eversion.

title: Return to Running After Tibial Bone Stress Injury: A Systematic Review
notes: Systematic review of return-to-running protocols and outcomes after tibial BSI, examining timelines, criteria, and recurrence rates.

title: Risk factors for metatarsal stress fractures: A systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: Systematic review and meta-analysis of risk factors specific to metatarsal stress fractures, including foot morphology, training load, and biomechanical factors.

title: Biomechanical factors associated with tibial stress fracture in female runners
journal: Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise
notes: Foundational study identifying higher vertical loading rate and tibial shock as biomechanical risk factors for tibial stress fractures in female runners.

title: Preventing Bone Stress Injuries in Runners with Optimal Workload
notes: Reviewed workload management strategies for preventing BSI in runners, including progressive loading principles, ACWR application, and bone adaptation timelines.

title: Management and Prevention of Bone Stress Injuries in Long-Distance Runners
journal: Journal of Orthopaedic & Sports Physical Therapy
notes: Comprehensive clinical review of BSI management in long-distance runners covering risk classification, imaging, treatment, and evidence-based return-to-running protocols.

title: Interval training at VO2max: effects on aerobic performance and overtraining markers
notes: Examined 4-week interval training at vVO2max in runners, measuring aerobic performance changes and overtraining markers.

title: Interval training for performance: a scientific and empirical practice. Special recommendations for middle- and long-distance running. Part I: aerobic interval training
notes: Comprehensive review of aerobic interval training science with specific recommendations for middle- and long-distance runners.

title: The scientific basis for high-intensity interval training: optimising training programmes and maximising performance in highly trained endurance athletes
notes: Reviewed the scientific basis for HIIT in highly trained athletes where additional submaximal volume no longer improves performance.

title: Is there an optimal training intensity for enhancing the maximal oxygen uptake of distance runners?: empirical research findings, current opinions, physiological rationale and practical recommendations
notes: Reviewed empirical evidence and physiological rationale for optimal training intensity to enhance VO2max in distance runners.

title: Aerobic high-intensity intervals improve VO2max more than moderate training
notes: RCT comparing four training protocols (LSD, LT, 15/15, 4x4min) in 40 moderately trained men showing 4x4min intervals most effective for VO2max.

title: Adaptations to aerobic interval training: interactive effects of exercise intensity and total work duration
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports
notes: RCT of 35 cyclists comparing 4x4min, 4x8min, and 4x16min interval durations to determine interaction of intensity and work duration.

title: High-intensity interval training, solutions to the programming puzzle: Part I: cardiopulmonary emphasis
notes: Part 1 of comprehensive HIIT programming framework addressing cardiopulmonary adaptations and work/rest manipulation variables.

title: High-intensity interval training, solutions to the programming puzzle. Part II: anaerobic energy, neuromuscular load and practical applications
notes: Part 2 of HIIT programming framework covering anaerobic energy systems, neuromuscular load, and practical training applications.

title: Effectiveness of High-Intensity Interval Training (HIT) and Continuous Endurance Training for VO2max Improvements: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Controlled Trials
notes: Meta-analysis of 28 controlled trials comparing HIIT vs continuous endurance training for VO2max improvements.

title: Six Sessions of Sprint Interval Training Improves Running Performance in Trained Athletes
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: Examined whether just six sprint interval training sessions could improve 3km time trial and other performance measures in trained trail runners.

title: Physiological basis of interval training for performance enhancement
notes: Review of the physiological mechanisms underlying interval training adaptations for performance enhancement.

title: Is iron treatment beneficial in, iron-deficient but non-anaemic (IDNA) endurance athletes? A systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analysis examining whether iron supplementation improves VO2max and exercise performance in iron-deficient but non-anaemic endurance athletes.

title: Effects of Oral Iron Supplementation on Blood Iron Status in Athletes: A Systematic Review, Meta-Analysis and Meta-Regression of Randomized Controlled Trials
notes: Meta-analysis of 13 RCTs examining effects of oral iron supplementation on blood iron parameters and physical performance in healthy adult athletes.

title: Does Iron Supplementation Improve Performance in Iron-Deficient Nonanemic Athletes?
notes: Systematic review evaluating whether iron supplementation improves athletic performance in iron-deficient nonanemic athletes.

title: Iron deficiency in sports - definition, influence on performance and therapy
notes: Swiss Society of Sports Medicine consensus statement providing diagnostic criteria and treatment guidelines for iron deficiency in athletes.

title: Iron Status and Physical Performance in Athletes
notes: Comprehensive review of iron metabolism, prevalence of iron deficiency in athletes, and the relationship between iron status and physical performance.

title: Athletic induced iron deficiency: new insights into the role of inflammation, cytokines and hormones
notes: Review examining the mechanisms of exercise-induced iron deficiency including the roles of inflammation, hepcidin, cytokines, and hormones in athletes.

title: Footstrike is the major cause of hemolysis during running
notes: Experimental study comparing plasma free hemoglobin after uphill vs downhill treadmill running to isolate footstrike as the primary mechanism of exercise-induced hemolysis.

title: Foot-strike Hemolysis: A Scoping Review of Long-Distance Runners
notes: Scoping review synthesizing evidence on foot-strike hemolysis prevalence, mechanisms, and clinical significance in long-distance runners.

title: Bone Health in Athletes: The Role of Exercise, Nutrition, and Hormones
notes: Review examining the interplay of exercise type, calcium, vitamin D, energy availability, and hormonal status on bone mineral density and stress fracture risk in athletes.

title: Vitamin D and Stress Fractures in Sport: Preventive and Therapeutic Measures-A Narrative Review
notes: Narrative review examining the relationship between vitamin D status and stress fracture incidence in athletes, with preventive and therapeutic recommendations.

title: Calcium and vitamin D supplementation decreases incidence of stress fractures in female navy recruits
journal: Journal of Bone and Mineral Research
notes: Randomized double-blind placebo-controlled trial showing 2000mg calcium plus 800IU vitamin D daily reduced stress fracture incidence by 20% in female navy recruits during basic training.

title: The significance of the aerobic-anaerobic transition for the determination of work load intensities during endurance training
journal: European Journal of Applied Physiology and Occupational Physiology
notes: Examined aerobic and anaerobic thresholds (4 mmol/L lactate) in cross-country skiers to determine workload intensities for endurance training prescription.

title: Justification of the 4-mmol/l lactate threshold
notes: Provided the scientific justification for using a fixed 4 mmol/L blood lactate concentration as the onset of blood lactate accumulation (OBLA) threshold.

title: Blood lactate parameters related to aerobic capacity and endurance performance
journal: European Journal of Applied Physiology and Occupational Physiology
notes: Compared four blood lactate threshold descriptors (LT, LT1, LT2, OBLA) against aerobic capacity and 12-minute running performance in 19 untrained females.

title: Anaerobic threshold: the concept and methods of measurement
notes: Reviewed the anaerobic threshold concept and measurement methods including MLSS, lactate minimum test, LT, OBLA, individual anaerobic threshold, and ventilatory threshold.

title: Blood lactate measurements and analysis during exercise: a guide for clinicians
journal: Journal of Diabetes Science and Technology
notes: Reviewed practical blood lactate measurement and analysis methods during exercise for clinical and athletic performance testing applications.

title: Lactate threshold concepts: how valid are they?
notes: Critically reviewed 25 different lactate threshold concepts evaluating their physiological validity, repeatability, and practical application for performance prediction.

title: Reliability of maximal lactate-steady-state
notes: Examined the test-retest reliability of MLSS determination on cycle ergometry in 32 male subjects, finding 3% coefficient of variability for power output.

title: Repeatability and predictive value of lactate threshold concepts in endurance sports
notes: Compared repeatability and predictive value of multiple lactate threshold concepts for endurance performance, finding Dmax-mod most valid for MLSS estimation.

title: Manipulating graded exercise test variables affects the validity of the lactate threshold and VO2peak
notes: Examined how varying GXT stage length (1-10 min) affects 14 different lactate threshold calculations, finding only 11 of 58 threshold values validly estimated MLSS.

title: An Examination and Critique of Current Methods to Determine Exercise Intensity
notes: Critically examined current methods for determining and prescribing exercise intensity, noting lack of agreed framework for assessing construct validity of threshold concepts.

title: Biochemical adaptations in muscle. Effects of exercise on mitochondrial oxygen uptake and respiratory enzyme activity in skeletal muscle
notes: Foundational study demonstrating that endurance exercise training doubles mitochondrial oxidative capacity in skeletal muscle (rat model).

title: Regulation of mitochondrial biogenesis in muscle by endurance exercise
notes: Reviewed molecular mechanisms by which endurance exercise regulates mitochondrial biogenesis in skeletal muscle.

title: Physiological regulation of marathon performance
notes: Reviewed the physiological factors regulating marathon performance including aerobic metabolism, carbohydrate stores, and thermoregulation.

title: Metabolic factors limiting performance in marathon runners
notes: Developed a mathematical model of carbohydrate depletion (hitting the wall) during marathon running based on personalized physiological parameters.

title: New insights into the interaction of carbohydrate and fat metabolism during exercise
notes: Reviewed how carbohydrate and fat metabolism interact during exercise and how training alters fuel selection.

title: Maximizing Cellular Adaptation to Endurance Exercise in Skeletal Muscle
notes: Reviewed molecular signaling networks underlying skeletal muscle adaptation to endurance exercise including nutrient-exercise interactions.

title: Regulation of Muscle Glycogen Metabolism during Exercise: Implications for Endurance Performance and Training Adaptations
