notes: Reviewed regulation of glycogen metabolism during exercise and implications for endurance performance and training adaptations.

title: Adaptations to Endurance and Strength Training
journal: Cold Spring Harbor Perspectives in Medicine
notes: Reviewed molecular and cellular mechanisms of adaptation to endurance and strength training including mitochondrial biogenesis and angiogenesis.

title: Training for a (half-)marathon: Training volume and longest endurance run related to performance and running injuries
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports
notes: Prospective study of 997 marathon/half-marathon runners examining associations between training volume, longest run, performance, and injury.

title: An evaluation of the training determinants of marathon performance: A meta-analysis with meta-regression
journal: Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport
notes: Meta-analysis with meta-regression of 85 studies examining dose-response relationships between training behaviors and marathon finish time.

title: Effects of tapering on performance in endurance athletes: A systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: Systematically reviewed and meta-analyzed 14 studies on tapering strategies evaluating effects of taper duration, volume reduction magnitude, and intensity maintenance on performance.

title: Effects of tapering on performance: a meta-analysis
journal: Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise
notes: Meta-analyzed 27 studies to determine optimal taper characteristics (duration, volume reduction, intensity, frequency) for maximizing competition performance.

title: Pacing strategies in marathons: A systematic review
notes: Systematically reviewed 39 studies on marathon pacing strategies across performance levels, sex, age groups, and environmental conditions.

title: The Training Intensity Distribution of Marathon Runners Across Performance Levels
notes: Analyzed training intensity distribution across 119,452 marathon runners of varying performance levels, quantifying zone-specific volume and its relationship to finish time.

title: Interactions between monthly training volume, frequency and running distance per workout on marathon time
notes: Investigated interactions between monthly training volume, frequency, and per-session distance as predictors of marathon finish time in 494 recreational male runners.

title: Longer Disciplined Tapers Improve Marathon Performance for Recreational Runners
journal: Frontiers in Sports and Active Living
notes: Analyzed taper behavior of 158,000+ recreational marathon runners to quantify the relationship between taper duration, discipline, and finish-time outcomes.

title: The Determinants of Marathon Performance: An Observational Analysis of Anthropometric, Pre-race and In-race Variables
notes: Measured anthropometric, pre-race training, and in-race pacing variables in 260 marathon runners to determine which factors explain greatest variance in performance.

title: Quantitative Analysis of 92 12-Week Sub-elite Marathon Training Plans
notes: Quantitatively analyzed 92 published 12-week marathon training plans for sub-elite runners, extracting weekly volume, intensity distribution, long run prescription, and periodization patterns.

title: Improved marathon performance by in-race nutritional strategy intervention
journal: International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism
notes: Compared marathon finish times between runners using a prescribed in-race nutritional strategy versus self-selected nutrition during the same event.

title: Meta-analysis of the age-associated decline in maximal aerobic capacity in men: relation to training status
journal: American Journal of Physiology - Heart and Circulatory Physiology
notes: Landmark meta-analysis of 242 studies (538 groups, 13,828 subjects) examining VO2max decline across sedentary, active, and endurance-trained men. Found absolute decline rates of ~4.0-4.6 ml/kg/min/decade across all groups, with relative declines of 6.8-8.7% per decade. Challenges the hypothesis that training status affects the rate of age-related VO2max decline.

title: Decline in VO2max with aging in master athletes and sedentary men
notes: Longitudinal study over ~8 years comparing 15 master athletes (mean age 62) vs 14 sedentary controls (mean age 61). Trained athletes declined 5.5% per decade (2.2 ml/kg/min) vs 12% per decade (3.3 ml/kg/min) in sedentary. Demonstrates that continued endurance training halves the rate of VO2max decline with aging.

title: A longitudinal assessment of change in VO2max and maximal heart rate in master athletes
journal: Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise
notes: Longitudinal study tracking 86 male and 49 female master endurance runners over ~8.5 years. VO2max declined 1-4.6% annually in men and 0.5-2.4% in women. Lean body mass maintenance correlated with VO2max preservation in men; estrogen replacement and sustained training volume associated with maintained VO2max in women. Important sex-specific findings for masters training.

title: Endurance exercise performance in Masters athletes: age-associated changes and underlying physiological mechanisms
notes: Comprehensive review describing that peak endurance performance is maintained until ~35 years, with modest decreases until 50-60 and progressively steeper declines thereafter. VO2max decline is the primary driver, but reductions in lactate threshold and training intensity/volume also contribute. Foundational reference for understanding age-performance physiology in masters endurance athletes.

title: The Age-Related Performance Decline in Marathon Running: The Paradigm of the Berlin Marathon
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
notes: Massive cohort study of 387,222 Berlin Marathon finishers (2008-2018). Peak performance at age 32 (women) and 34 (men). Runners at 60-64 maintained ~90% of peak running speed. Performance decline accelerated after peak age, with sex-based differences in the aging trajectory. Provides the largest dataset quantifying age-related marathon performance decline.

title: Chronic exercise preserves lean muscle mass in masters athletes
notes: Cross-sectional study of 40 masters athletes aged 40-81 training 4-5 times/week with MRI of quadriceps. Mid-thigh muscle area and lean mass did NOT decline with age. Challenges the assumption that sarcopenia is inevitable; suggests muscle loss reflects chronic disuse rather than aging alone. The iconic MRI comparison images from this study are widely referenced.

title: Different Training Modalities Improve Energy Cost and Performance in Master Runners
notes: RCT comparing continuous moderate-intensity (CMIT), discontinuous high-intensity (DHIT), and control in 34 male masters runners (mean age 47.2). Both CMIT and DHIT improved running economy (4-5%), gas exchange threshold speed (3-6%), and 5-km performance (2-3%) over 8 weeks. Neither affected VO2peak. Demonstrates masters runners retain significant trainability with structured interventions.

title: Sub 3-Hour Marathon Runners for Five Consecutive Decades Demonstrate a Reduced Age-Related Decline in Performance
notes: Longitudinal study of 40 sub-3-hour marathoners tracked across five decades (1970s-2010s). With consistent training, age-related marathon performance decline was limited to <7% per decade until age 60. Mean decline was ~1:04/year (0.67% annual decrease). Demonstrates that sustained high-level training dramatically attenuates performance losses in masters marathoners.

title: Muscle morphology and performance in master athletes: A systematic review and meta-analyses
notes: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 55 studies on muscle morphology and physical function in masters athletes. Master endurance athletes maintained VO2max values comparable to young healthy controls (42.0 vs 43.1 ml/kg/min). Chronic exercise training preserves physical function, muscular strength, and body fat levels in a mode-specific manner. Key reference for understanding how training type affects age-related muscle changes.

title: Musculoskeletal injury in the masters runners
notes: Retrospective survey of 2,886 runners examining injury patterns by age. Overall injury rate was 46%, with masters runners (40+) experiencing more multiple injuries and greater prevalence of soft tissue injuries (calf, Achilles, hamstring). Knee and foot injuries most common in both groups. One of few studies specifically comparing injury epidemiology between masters and younger runners.

title: Master Athletes Are Extending the Limits of Human Endurance
notes: Review documenting the increasing participation and improving performance trends among masters athletes (40+) in endurance events. VO2max is the parameter most altered by age, while exercise economy and lactate threshold show lesser declines. Sustaining high training intensity with advancing age is the most significant factor limiting performance deterioration. Masters athletes positioned as models for healthy aging.

title: Lifelong Endurance Exercise as a Countermeasure Against Age-Related VO2max Decline: Physiological Overview and Insights from Masters Athletes
notes: Comprehensive review showing masters athletes who maintain training volume can limit VO2max decline to ~5-6.5% per decade, vs up to 46% per decade for those who become sedentary. Training volume explained 54% (men) and 39% (women) of variance in VO2max decline. Provides a multi-systemic overview of how lifelong endurance exercise attenuates age-related cardiorespiratory deterioration.

title: Psychological Determinants of Whole-Body Endurance Performance
notes: Landmark systematic review identifying psychological interventions that improve endurance performance. Found consistent support for imagery, self-talk, and goal setting. Also identified mental fatigue as undermining performance and verbal encouragement/competition as beneficial. Essential reference for any coaching AI needing to understand which mental strategies have evidence behind them.

title: Self-Talk and Sports Performance: A Meta-Analysis
notes: Meta-analysis of 32 studies (62 effect sizes) establishing the effectiveness of self-talk in sport with a positive moderate effect size (ES = 0.48). Found self-talk more effective for fine motor tasks than gross motor tasks, and for novel vs well-learned tasks. Instructional self-talk was more effective for fine motor tasks while motivational self-talk worked for both. Foundational evidence that self-talk strategies should be part of any coaching toolkit.

title: Talking yourself out of exhaustion: the effects of self-talk on endurance performance
notes: First RCT to demonstrate that motivational self-talk significantly reduces perceived exertion and enhances endurance performance. Participants using self-talk increased time-to-exhaustion from 637 to 750 seconds (18% improvement) with reduced RPE at 50% isotime. Grounded in the psychobiological model, this directly shows that coaching runners to use motivational self-talk cues can meaningfully improve race outcomes.

title: Self-talk: Review and sport-specific model
notes: Introduces a sport-specific model of self-talk based on dual-process theory distinguishing System 1 (automatic, emotionally driven) from System 2 (deliberate, controlled) self-talk. Identifies five key components critical to self-talk interventions. Provides the theoretical framework for understanding how and when self-talk interventions work, enabling a coaching AI to recommend appropriate self-talk strategies for different racing situations.

title: Attentional focus in endurance activity: new paradigms and future directions
journal: International Review of Sport and Exercise Psychology
notes: Comprehensive review of 35+ years of research on association vs dissociation in endurance activity. Key finding: elite athletes tend to perform better with associative (body-monitoring) focus, while recreational athletes prefer and benefit from dissociative strategies. Proposes a new working model of attentional focus that goes beyond the simple association/dissociation dichotomy. Critical for advising runners on what to focus on during races and training.

title: Metacognitive processes in the self-regulation of performance in elite endurance runners
notes: Qualitative interview study with 10 elite endurance runners revealing that metacognitive strategies (planning, monitoring, reviewing, evaluating) are central to cognitive control during racing. Developed an integrative metacognitive framework incorporating attentional focus dimensions. Directly applicable to coaching: elite runners actively plan and adjust their mental strategies throughout a race rather than passively experiencing thoughts.

title: Mental fatigue impairs physical performance in humans
notes: Landmark RCT demonstrating that 90 minutes of demanding cognitive work reduced subsequent cycling time-to-exhaustion by 15% (640s vs 754s control) despite no differences in cardiorespiratory or musculoenergetic responses. The effect was mediated entirely by higher perceived exertion. Foundational paper for the psychobiological model; directly relevant to coaching advice about pre-race mental preparation, avoiding cognitive drain before competition.

title: The psychobiological model of endurance performance: an effort-based decision-making theory to explain self-paced endurance performance
notes: Theoretical paper presenting the psychobiological model of endurance performance, proposing that pace regulation is an effort-based decision-making process where performance termination occurs when perceived effort exceeds motivation. Integrates perception of effort, potential motivation, knowledge of distance, and prior experience into a unified framework. Essential theoretical foundation for understanding why psychological interventions work in endurance sport.

title: Effects of mental toughness on athletic performance: a systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: International Journal of Sport and Exercise Psychology
notes: Meta-analysis of 16 studies finding a moderate-to-high correlation between mental toughness and athletic performance (r = 0.36). Key moderator finding: the relationship was strongest in combat sports (r = 0.73), moderate in ball sports (r = 0.30), and similar in endurance sports (r = 0.32). Adults showed stronger effects than adolescents (r = 0.41 vs 0.20). Confirms mental toughness is a meaningful predictor but not the sole psychological factor for endurance success.

title: Pacing Decision Making in Sport and the Effects of Interpersonal Competition: A Critical Review
notes: Critical review of 75 papers (2000-2017) on how opponents and competition influence pacing decisions. Found that competitor behavior is an essential determinant of exercise intensity regulation, but athletes' responses vary by competitive context and physical state. Emphasizes that pacing is a complex decision-making process integrating both internal (fatigue, effort) and external (opponents, environment) factors. Directly relevant for race strategy advice in coached running.

title: Nutrition and Supplement Update for the Endurance Athlete: Review and Recommendations
notes: Reviewed and updated macronutrient, hydration, and supplement recommendations (nitrates, caffeine, antioxidants, probiotics) for endurance athletes.

title: Nutrition for endurance sports: marathon, triathlon, and road cycling
notes: Reviewed carbohydrate, fluid, and sodium strategies for marathon, triathlon, and road cycling with individualized fueling guidelines by exercise duration.

title: Protein supplementation increases adaptations to endurance training: A systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analyzed effects of protein supplementation combined with endurance training on aerobic capacity, lean mass, and time trial performance.

title: Dietary Intake of Recreational Endurance Runners Associated with Race Distance-Results from the NURMI Study (Step 2)
notes: Surveyed dietary intake of 211 recreational endurance runners across 10-km, half-marathon, and marathon/ultra-marathon distance groups using food frequency questionnaires.

title: Nutritional Intake and Energy Availability of Collegiate Distance Runners
journal: Journal of the American College of Nutrition
notes: Assessed dietary intake and energy availability of 41 collegiate distance runners, finding inadequate carbohydrate, calcium, and vitamin D intake relative to training demands.

title: Low Energy Availability and Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analyzed 46 studies on prevalence of low energy availability (44.7% of athletes) and its associations with bone health, performance, and metabolic markers across sports.

title: Iron and the endurance athlete
notes: Reviewed iron's role in endurance performance, mechanisms of exercise-induced iron loss (hepcidin, hemolysis), prevalence of deficiency, and screening/supplementation guidelines.

title: Effects of Caffeine Intake on Endurance Running Performance and Time to Exhaustion: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
notes: Meta-analyzed 21 RCTs examining caffeine's ergogenic effect on endurance running time-to-exhaustion and time-trial performance across recreational and trained runners.

title: Effects of Beetroot Juice Supplementation on Cardiorespiratory Endurance in Athletes. A Systematic Review
notes: Systematically reviewed 23 studies on beetroot juice supplementation and its effects on cardiorespiratory endurance performance via nitrate-nitric oxide pathway.

title: Diagnosing Overtraining Syndrome: A Scoping Review
notes: Scoping review of 39 studies evaluating diagnostic markers and scoring systems for overtraining syndrome.

title: Overtraining Syndrome Symptoms and Diagnosis in Athletes: Where Is the Research? A Systematic Review
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Systematic review finding zero studies providing objective evidence of detailed performance changes from prior to OTS diagnosis.

title: Overtraining Syndrome: A Practical Guide
notes: Clinical review of OTS pathophysiology hypotheses (glycogen, serotonin, inflammation), diagnosis as exclusion, and practical prevention strategies.

title: Overtraining Syndrome as a Complex Systems Phenomenon
notes: Proposed a complex systems framework for OTS, advocating trans-omics and machine learning over single-biomarker approaches.

title: Improving the Diagnosis of Nonfunctional Overreaching and Overtraining Syndrome
notes: Used discriminant analysis on hormonal responses during two-bout exercise protocol to differentiate NFO from OTS with 98% sensitivity.

title: Monitoring training in athletes with reference to overtraining syndrome
notes: Introduced session-RPE method and training monotony/strain calculations, showing illness correlated with individually identifiable training thresholds.

title: Monitoring training load to understand fatigue in athletes
notes: Reviewed external and internal training load monitoring methods and their practical implementation for preventing non-functional overreaching.

title: Preventing overtraining in athletes in high-intensity sports and stress/recovery monitoring
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports
notes: Reviewed stress-recovery monitoring instruments and their role in balancing training load with recovery to prevent overtraining.

title: Novel insights of overtraining syndrome discovered from the EROS study
notes: Compared 67 clinical and hormonal parameters across OTS athletes, healthy athletes, and sedentary controls, identifying 'paradoxical deconditioning' as a key mechanism.

title: Training-overtraining. A prospective, experimental study with experienced middle- and long-distance runners
notes: Prospective study doubling training volume (86 to 175 km/week) in experienced runners to induce overtraining, monitoring cardiovascular, metabolic, and hormonal parameters.

title: Comparative Effects of Pacing Strategies on Endurance Performance: A Systematic Review and Network Meta-Analysis
notes: Network meta-analysis of 24 studies (302 participants, 765 observations) comparing imposed pacing strategies (fast-start, even, slow-start) against self-selected pacing. Found no significant differences between self-selected and imposed strategies across the network. Key implication: experienced athletes' self-selected pacing may already be near-optimal, and forcing a different strategy does not reliably improve performance.

title: Describing and understanding pacing strategies during athletic competition
notes: Landmark taxonomy paper that defined the six canonical pacing profiles: negative, all-out, positive, even, parabolic (J/U-shaped), and variable. Established that short events (<30s) favor all-out strategies while prolonged events (>2min) benefit from even pacing. Foundational reference for all subsequent pacing research; still the most-cited pacing strategy paper in sport science.

title: The physiology and psychology of negative splits: insights into optimal marathon pacing strategies
notes: Mini-review integrating physiological and psychological mechanisms behind negative-split marathon pacing. Covers glycogen sparing, attenuated cardiovascular drift, improved thermoregulation, and reduced lactate accumulation from conservative early pacing. Also discusses training modalities (progressive long runs, fast-finish workouts) and psychological tools (goal-setting, visualization) to develop negative-split capability. Directly actionable for a running coach AI.

title: How recreational marathon runners hit the wall: A large-scale data analysis of late-race pacing collapse in the marathon
notes: Massive data analysis of 4+ million marathon race records defining 'hitting the wall' via pacing data. Found 28% of men and 17% of women experience sustained late-race pacing collapse. Slowdowns more frequent in the 3 years around a personal best (36% vs 23% in other years), suggesting runners push harder near PBs. Critical evidence for coaching recreational runners on pacing discipline and wall-avoidance strategies.

title: Fast starters and slow finishers: A large-scale data analysis of pacing at the beginning and end of the marathon for recreational runners
notes: Analysis of 1.7 million recreational marathon performances examining pacing at race start and finish. Found fast starts tend to be very fast leading to endurance problems, while fast finishes suggest overly cautious earlier pacing. Women pace more effectively than men but can be too cautious, costing minutes overall. Provides empirical evidence that even or slightly positive splits optimize recreational marathon performance.

title: Pacing profiles and tactical behaviors of elite runners
journal: Journal of Sport and Health Science
notes: Comprehensive review of pacing behaviors across all running distances from 100m to marathon in elite competition. All-out strategy in sprints; U-shaped pacing in middle-distance world records; negative pacing with micro-variations in 5000m/10000m championship races. Distinguishes championship racing (tactical, variable) from time-trial/record attempts (even pacing). Essential reference for understanding distance-specific pacing strategies.

title: Real-time feedback by wearables in running: Current approaches, challenges and suggestions for improvements
notes: Review of how wearable devices (GPS watches, accelerometers) provide real-time feedback on pace, biomechanics, and workload during running. Discusses how feedback modality, frequency, and timing affect pacing behavior and performance. A mix of real-time and delayed feedback is most effective. Directly relevant to coaching AI that integrates GPS/wearable data for pacing guidance and feedback design.

title: New horizons for the methodology and physiology of training periodization
notes: Reviewed limitations of traditional periodization and proposed block periodization as an alternative framework using specialized mesocycle-blocks with concentrated training workloads for high-performance athletes.

title: Block periodization of endurance training - a systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: Open Access Journal of Sports Medicine
notes: First meta-analysis of block vs traditional periodization in endurance training, screening 2,905 studies and pooling 6 for meta-analysis of VO2max and Wmax outcomes.

title: Effects of 16 weeks of pyramidal and polarized training intensity distributions in well-trained endurance runners
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine and Science in Sports
notes: Randomized 60 well-trained male runners to four 16-week periodization arms (pyramidal, polarized, pyramidal-to-polarized, polarized-to-pyramidal) and measured VO2peak, lactate thresholds, and 5-km time trial performance.

title: Block periodization of high-intensity aerobic intervals provides superior training effects in trained cyclists
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine and Science in Sports
notes: Compared 4-week block periodization (concentrated HIT sessions) vs traditional organization (evenly distributed HIT) in trained cyclists, measuring VO2max, Wmax, and power at 2 mmol/L lactate.

title: Effects of 12 weeks of block periodization on performance and performance indices in well-trained cyclists
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine and Science in Sports
notes: Compared 12 weeks of block periodization (5 HIT sessions every 4th week) vs traditional training (2 weekly HIT sessions) in 15 well-trained cyclists.

title: No Differences Between 12 Weeks of Block- vs. Traditional-Periodized Training in Performance Adaptations in Trained Cyclists
notes: Randomized 30 trained cyclists to 12 weeks of block vs traditional periodization and found equivalent time-trial performance improvements but differing hematological and capillary adaptations.

title: The Effect of Periodization and Training Intensity Distribution on Middle- and Long-Distance Running Performance: A Systematic Review
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Systematically reviewed 16 studies on training intensity distribution approaches (pyramidal, polarized, threshold) specifically in middle- and long-distance runners.

title: Training Periodization, Methods, Intensity Distribution, and Volume in Highly Trained and Elite Distance Runners: A Systematic Review
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Systematically reviewed periodization patterns, training methods, intensity distribution, and volume characteristics used by highly trained and elite middle- and long-distance runners.

title: Block periodization versus traditional training theory: a review
journal: The Journal of Sports Medicine and Physical Fitness
notes: Foundational review of major contradictions between traditional periodization theory and high-performance sport demands, introducing rationale for block periodization. Pre-DOI era Minerva Medica publication.

title: Quantifying training intensity distribution in elite endurance athletes: is there evidence for an "optimal" distribution?
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine & Science in Sports
notes: Quantified daily training intensity distribution in junior cross-country skiers using three different methods across 32 days.

title: The training intensity distribution among well-trained and elite endurance athletes
notes: Review analyzing retrospective training data of elite endurance athletes to characterize pyramidal and polarized TID patterns.

title: Polarized vs. Threshold Training Intensity Distribution on Endurance Sport Performance: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: Meta-analysis of RCTs comparing polarized vs threshold training on endurance performance outcomes.

title: Comparison of Polarized Versus Other Types of Endurance Training Intensity Distribution on Athletes' Endurance Performance: A Systematic Review with Meta-analysis
notes: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 17 studies (n=437) comparing polarized TID vs other distributions on endurance performance.

title: Which Training Intensity Distribution Intervention will Produce the Greatest Improvements in Maximal Oxygen Uptake and Time-Trial Performance in Endurance Athletes? A Systematic Review and Network Meta-analysis of Individual Participant Data
notes: Network meta-analysis of individual participant data comparing all major TID interventions on VO2max and time-trial performance.

title: Protein Nutrition for Endurance Athletes: A Metabolic Focus on Promoting Recovery and Training Adaptation
notes: Reviewed metabolic rationale for protein recommendations in endurance athletes, proposing ~1.8 g/kg/day with higher intakes during carbohydrate-restricted training.

title: Protein Requirements Are Elevated in Endurance Athletes after Exercise as Determined by the Indicator Amino Acid Oxidation Method
notes: Used the indicator amino acid oxidation method to determine protein requirements in endurance-trained men during a controlled 3-day training period.

title: Dose-response effects of dietary protein on muscle protein synthesis during recovery from endurance exercise in young men: a double-blind randomized trial
notes: Measured dose-response effects of 0-45g protein on muscle protein synthesis rates during recovery from endurance exercise in trained young men.

title: International Society of Sports Nutrition Position Stand: protein and exercise
journal: Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition
notes: Published the ISSN consensus position on protein intake recommendations for physically active individuals, covering dosage, timing, source, and quality.

title: Effects of Protein Supplementation on Performance and Recovery in Resistance and Endurance Training
notes: Reviewed evidence on peri-exercise protein supplementation effects on performance and recovery in both resistance and endurance training contexts.

title: Protein and the Adaptive Response With Endurance Training: Wishful Thinking or a Competitive Edge?
notes: Examined whether dietary protein augments endurance-specific training adaptations including mitochondrial biogenesis, capillarization, and oxygen-transport protein synthesis.

title: Animal Protein versus Plant Protein in Supporting Lean Mass and Muscle Strength: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis of Randomized Controlled Trials
notes: Meta-analyzed RCTs comparing animal vs plant protein sources on lean mass and muscle strength outcomes.

title: Athletic Records and Human Endurance
notes: The foundational paper introducing the Riegel formula T2 = T1 * (D2/D1)^1.06 for predicting race times across distances in the endurance range (3.5-230 minutes). Based on analysis of world records across running, swimming, and walking. Remains the most widely used race prediction formula in running calculators worldwide, though later work shows it is too optimistic for recreational runners at marathon distance.

title: An empirical study of race times in recreational endurance runners
journal: BMC Sports Science, Medicine and Rehabilitation
notes: Analyzed 2,303 recreational runners and found that the Riegel formula underestimated marathon times by at least 10 minutes for half the participants. Developed improved prediction models incorporating sex, age, BMI, and training variables (tempo runs, weekly mileage, intervals) that achieved mean squared errors of 208-228 vs Riegel's 381. Critical evidence that classical formulas calibrated on elite runners perform poorly for recreational athletes.

title: An Evaluation of Time-Trial-Based Predictions of VO2max and Recommended Training Paces for Collegiate and Recreational Runners
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: Only direct validation study of the Daniels VDOT system against laboratory-measured VO2max. Tested NCAA Division 1 athletes (n=11) and recreational runners (n=9). VDOT underestimated VO2max in both groups, with larger discrepancies in recreational runners. Threshold pace recommendations were reliable, but interval pace may not be optimal for improving VO2max in recreational runners. Key caution for coaching AI systems relying on VDOT-derived training paces.

title: A minimal power model for human running performance
notes: Derives the observed logarithmic scaling between world record running speeds and times from first principles of metabolic power supply. The model describes female and male record performances from 800m to marathon with mean errors of less than 1%. Introduces a quantitative definition of endurance using a relative metabolic power scale, providing a physiological basis for race prediction superior to purely empirical formulas like Riegel's.

title: Human running performance from real-world big data
notes: Applied a physiological power model to ~1.6 million running sessions from ~14,000 individuals, achieving race time predictions with mean error of only 2%. Uses two parameters — aerobic power index and endurance index — to characterize individual runners. The endurance parameter enables identification of lactate threshold from training data alone. Demonstrates that combining physiological models with big training data produces far more accurate individual predictions than population-based formulas.

title: Recommendations for marathon runners: on the application of recommender systems and machine learning to support recreational marathon runners
notes: Large-scale study using 1.86 million Strava training and race activities from 31,221 marathon runners (Dublin, London, New York 2014-2017). Best gradient-boosted model predicted marathon times within 14.34 minutes (<6% error). Demonstrates case-based reasoning for personalized training recommendations by matching runners to similar historical profiles. Directly relevant to building a Strava-based coaching AI that generates race predictions from training logs.

title: Prediction of Marathon Performance using Artificial Intelligence
notes: Compared k-nearest neighbor (KNN) and artificial neural network (ANN) for marathon prediction using 10km race time, BMI, age, and sex as inputs from 820 French runners. KNN achieved mean absolute error of 2.4% vs ANN's 5.6%, with both showing very high correlation with actual times (r>0.90). Demonstrates that simple ML models with minimal inputs (a recent 10K time plus basic demographics) can predict marathon performance with clinically useful accuracy for coaching applications.

title: Impact of Cold-Water Immersion Compared with Passive Recovery Following a Single Bout of Strenuous Exercise on Athletic Performance in Physically Active Participants: A Systematic Review with Meta-analysis and Meta-regression
notes: 52-study meta-analysis comparing CWI vs passive recovery. CWI improved muscular power recovery at 24h post eccentric and high-intensity exercise, reduced CK and muscle soreness, and improved perceived recovery. However, no significant improvement in strength performance. Shorter durations and lower temperatures were more effective. Key reference for evidence-based CWI protocols.

title: Effects of Cold-Water Immersion Compared with Other Recovery Modalities on Athletic Performance Following Acute Strenuous Exercise in Physically Active Participants: A Systematic Review, Meta-Analysis, and Meta-Regression
notes: 28-study meta-analysis comparing CWI against other recovery modalities (not just passive). CWI was superior for reducing muscle soreness but equivalent to other methods for power and flexibility recovery. Air cryotherapy outperformed CWI for strength recovery. Temperature and duration were rarely significant moderators. Important companion paper to Moore 2022 for understanding where CWI fits relative to alternatives.

title: Compression Garments and Recovery from Exercise: A Meta-Analysis
notes: 23-study meta-analysis of compression garments across resistance, running, and metabolic exercise. Found small but likely benefits (ES 0.38). Greatest benefits for strength recovery at 2-8h and >24h post-exercise. Compression most effective after resistance exercise at >24h timepoints. Pressure levels and training status did not significantly influence outcomes. Key reference for evidence-based compression use in recovery.

title: Are compression garments effective for the recovery of exercise-induced muscle damage? A systematic review with meta-analysis
notes: Systematic review with meta-analysis on compression garments for exercise-induced muscle damage. CK levels were unaffected, blood lactate unexpectedly increased, but compression reduced muscle swelling and perception of soreness. Showed faster recovery of muscle function after exercise. Important for understanding that compression benefits are primarily perceptual and functional rather than biochemical.

title: Massage and Performance Recovery: A Meta-Analytical Review
notes: 22-RCT meta-analysis on massage for athletic recovery. Effects small and partly unclear but can be relevant: shorter massage (5-12 min) had larger effects (+6.6%) than longer (>12 min, +1.0%). Short-term recovery (<10 min) showed bigger benefits (+7.9%) than longer periods. Untrained subjects benefited more (+6.5%) than trained athletes (+2.3%). Key insight: massage timing and duration matter more than technique.

title: Effect of sports massage on performance and recovery: a systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: 29-study meta-analysis (1012 participants) finding no evidence that massage improves strength, jump, sprint, endurance, or fatigue measures. However, found small but statistically significant improvements in flexibility and DOMS. Important for coaches: massage is primarily a soreness/perception tool, not a performance enhancer. Open access in BMJ.

title: A Systematic Review on the Effectiveness of Active Recovery Interventions on Athletic Performance of Professional-, Collegiate-, and Competitive-Level Adult Athletes
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: 26-study systematic review (471 subjects) on active recovery in competitive athletes. Sessions of 6-10 minutes showed consistently positive effects on performance. Blood lactate clearance was unreliable as a recovery marker. Positive psychological outcomes from active recovery but weak evidence for performance benefits. Key takeaway: active recovery is psychologically beneficial and 6-10 min is the sweet spot.

title: Effectiveness of Recovery Strategies After Training and Competition in Endurance Athletes: An Umbrella Review
notes: 2024 umbrella review synthesizing 22 reviews (63 studies, 1100 endurance athletes) covering massage, active recovery, cryotherapy, contrast water therapy, compression garments, stretching, and electrostimulation. No recovery strategy showed consistent universal benefits. Compression garments and cryotherapy showed positive effects; massage showed no effect. Most comprehensive recent synthesis for endurance athletes. Essential reference for evidence-based recovery programming.

title: 2023 International Olympic Committee's (IOC) consensus statement on Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (REDs)
notes: The most current IOC consensus on REDs, updating the 2014 and 2018 statements. Introduces a new REDs conceptual model, the REDs Clinical Assessment Tool (CAT2), and a physiological model with primary and secondary indicators. Broadens RED-S beyond female athletes to all athletes and expands the health and performance consequences.

title: IOC consensus statement on relative energy deficiency in sport (RED-S): 2018 update
notes: 2018 update to the original 2014 IOC RED-S consensus. Provides updated evidence on the health and performance effects of low energy availability across multiple body systems (bone, endocrine, metabolic, cardiovascular, immunological, psychological). Introduces the RED-S Clinical Assessment Tool (RED-S CAT) and Return to Play models.

title: The IOC consensus statement: beyond the Female Athlete Triad--Relative Energy Deficiency in Sport (RED-S)
notes: The landmark paper that introduced the RED-S concept, expanding beyond the Female Athlete Triad to recognize that relative energy deficiency affects males and females across multiple physiological systems. Defined RED-S as impaired physiological function caused by relative energy deficiency including metabolic rate, menstrual function, bone health, immunity, protein synthesis, and cardiovascular health.

title: American College of Sports Medicine position stand. The female athlete triad
journal: Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise
notes: The foundational ACSM position stand defining the Female Athlete Triad as the interrelationship among energy availability, menstrual function, and bone mineral density. Each condition ranges on a spectrum from health to disease (eating disorders, amenorrhea, osteoporosis). Provided screening, diagnosis, prevention, and treatment guidelines that preceded the RED-S framework.

title: 2014 Female Athlete Triad Coalition consensus statement on treatment and return to play of the female athlete triad: 1st International Conference held in San Francisco, CA, May 2012, and 2nd International Conference held in Indianapolis, IN, May 2013
notes: Clinical consensus providing treatment and return-to-play guidelines for the Female Athlete Triad. Introduced a risk stratification point system for sport participation clearance decisions. Recommends multidisciplinary team management including physician, dietitian, and psychologist. Established clinical criteria for low-risk, moderate-risk, and high-risk categories.

title: Low Energy Availability Is Difficult to Assess but Outcomes Have Large Impact on Bone Injury Rates in Elite Distance Athletes
journal: International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism
notes: Studied elite distance athletes, reporting energy availability, metabolic/reproductive function, bone mineral density, and injury/illness rates. Found that while LEA is difficult to quantify accurately, outcomes of LEA (menstrual dysfunction, low BMD) have large impact on bone stress injury rates. Compared diagnostic criteria from the Female Athlete Triad, LEAF-Q, and RED-S tools.

title: Energy Availability in Athletics: Health, Performance, and Physique
journal: International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism
notes: Track-and-field-specific review of energy availability prevalence (18-58% in male and female athletes), with highest prevalence in endurance and jump events. Discusses LEA effects on testosterone, amenorrhea, bone health, and performance. Emphasizes early screening with tools like LEAF-Q and multidisciplinary treatment including sport physician, dietitian, physiologist, and psychologist.

title: Low Energy Availability in Athletes 2020: An Updated Narrative Review of Prevalence, Risk, Within-Day Energy Balance, Knowledge, and Impact on Sports Performance
notes: Comprehensive updated narrative review of LEA prevalence (22-58% across sports), risk factors, within-day energy deficiency patterns, athlete/coach knowledge gaps, and performance impacts. Highlights that within-day energy deficiency may be as important as overall daily energy availability, and that many athletes and coaches have poor knowledge of LEA/RED-S consequences.

title: Relative energy deficiency in sports (RED-S): elucidation of endocrine changes affecting the health of males and females
notes: Elucidates the sex-specific endocrine disruptions caused by RED-S. In females, energy deficiency suppresses GnRH pulsatility leading to hypoestrogenism and functional hypothalamic amenorrhea. In males, it reduces testosterone (Exercise-Hypogonadal Male Condition). Both pathways impair bone health. Highlights that male RED-S is under-recognized compared to females despite similar prevalence.

title: Criteria for Return to Running After Anterior Cruciate Ligament Reconstruction: A Scoping Review
notes: Analyzed 201 studies finding fewer than 1 in 5 used performance-based criteria for return-to-running decisions; median permitted RTR was 12 postoperative weeks.

title: Continued Sports Activity, Using a Pain-Monitoring Model, During Rehabilitation in Patients with Achilles Tendinopathy: A Randomized Controlled Study
notes: RCT comparing continued pain-monitored tendon-loading activity (running/jumping) vs 6-week rest during rehab; found no significant difference at 12 months, supporting continued activity with pain monitoring.

title: Comprehensive Return to Competitive Distance Running: A Clinical Commentary
notes: Proposed a six-phase training framework for returning injured competitive distance runners to full competition, including tools for psychological readiness assessment and aerobic capacity monitoring.

title: A Proposed Return-to-Sport Program for Patients With Midportion Achilles Tendinopathy: Rationale and Implementation
journal: Journal of Orthopaedic and Sports Physical Therapy
notes: Structured 4-phase return-to-sport framework (symptom management/load reduction, recovery, rebuilding, RTS) for Achilles tendinopathy, emphasizing exercise therapy with at least 3 months before considering alternatives.

title: Reintroduction of Running After Anterior Cruciate Ligament Reconstruction With a Hamstrings Graft: Can We Predict Short-Term Success?
notes: Evaluated a structured 10-session 2-week running reintroduction program post-ACLR; found IKDC subjective score was the only significant predictor of successful return to running.

title: Role of Antigravity Training in Rehabilitation and Return to Sport After Running Injuries
notes: Reviewed evidence for antigravity treadmill use in running injury rehab; established readiness criterion of pain-free running at >95% body weight support for >30 minutes before transitioning to ground running.

title: Criteria-Based Return to Sprinting Progression Following Lower Extremity Injury
journal: International Journal of Sports Physical Therapy
notes: First published structured 3-stage criteria-based progression specifically for return to sprinting, using objective criteria at each stage gate to reduce reinjury risk.

title: Running economy and distance running performance of highly trained athletes
notes: Demonstrated that running economy explained 65.4% of 10km performance variation among 12 elite runners with similar VO2max values.

title: Factors affecting running economy in trained distance runners
notes: Comprehensive review of physiological and biomechanical factors affecting running economy in trained distance runners.

title: Economy of running: beyond the measurement of oxygen uptake
notes: Compared running economy expressed as oxygen cost versus energy cost in 16 trained male distance runners across three submaximal speeds.

title: The valid measurement of running economy in runners
notes: Evaluated the validity of oxygen cost as a running economy measure by comparison with underlying energy cost in runners.

title: Running economy: measurement, norms, and determining factors
notes: Reviewed how running economy is defined, measured, and the physiological and biomechanical factors that determine it.

title: Strategies to improve running economy
notes: Reviewed acute and chronic interventions to improve running economy including endurance training, HIIT, resistance training, plyometrics, altitude, and nutrition.

title: Effects of Strength Training on Running Economy in Highly Trained Runners: A Systematic Review With Meta-Analysis of Controlled Trials
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: Meta-analysis of controlled trials showing strength training programs improve running economy in highly trained runners.

title: Metabolic effects of carbon-plated running shoes: a systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: Frontiers in Sports and Active Living
notes: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 14 crossover trials examining metabolic effects of carbon-plated vs non-plated running shoes.

title: The effect of footwear on running performance and running economy in distance runners
notes: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 19 studies (14 in meta-analysis) examining how shoe mass, cushioning, and stiffness affect running economy and performance.

title: A systematic review of the effect of running shoes on running economy, performance and biomechanics: analysis by brand and model
notes: Systematic review of 36 articles analyzing 61 shoe models across brands for effects on running economy, performance, and biomechanics.

title: Systematic Review of the Role of Footwear Constructions in Running Biomechanics: Implications for Running-Related Injury and Performance
journal: Journal of Sports Science and Medicine
notes: Systematic review of 63 studies examining how shoe constructions (midsole, heel-toe drop, minimalist design, lacing, heel flare) affect running biomechanics and injury.

title: A Comparison of the Energetic Cost of Running in Marathon Racing Shoes
notes: Compared energetic cost of running in Nike Vaporfly 4% prototypes versus two established marathon racing shoes in high-caliber runners.

title: A Randomized Crossover Study Investigating the Running Economy of Highly-Trained Male and Female Distance Runners in Marathon Racing Shoes versus Track Spikes
notes: Randomized crossover comparing running economy in Nike Vaporfly 4% vs track spikes vs Adidas Adios in highly-trained male and female distance runners.

title: Metabolic and performance responses of male runners wearing 3 types of footwear: Nike Vaporfly 4%, Saucony Endorphin racing flats, and their own shoes
journal: Journal of Sport and Health Science
notes: Randomized crossover comparing metabolic cost and 5-km time-trial performance in Nike Vaporfly 4%, Saucony Endorphin racing flats, and habitual shoes in recreational male runners.

title: Shoe Cushioning Influences the Running Injury Risk According to Body Mass: A Randomized Controlled Trial Involving 848 Recreational Runners
notes: RCT examining whether shoe cushioning level (soft vs hard) influences running injury risk, stratified by runner body mass.

title: Effects of a minimalist shoe on running economy and 5-km running performance
notes: Experimental crossover comparing running economy and 5-km time-trial performance in minimalist versus conventional running shoes in trained runners.

title: Technological advances in elite marathon performance
notes: Observational analysis of 3886 performances from top 50 male and female marathoners at four major marathons to quantify shoe technology effects on race times.

title: The Impact of Sleep Interventions on Athletic Performance: A Systematic Review
notes: Systematically reviewed 25 studies on sleep interventions (hygiene, naps, extension, light manipulation) and their effects on athletic performance.

title: How much does sleep deprivation impair endurance performance? A systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analyzed 31 studies (38 effect sizes) quantifying the moderate negative effect of sleep deprivation on endurance performance, with greater impairment in exercises >30 min.

title: Effects of Acute Sleep Loss on Physical Performance: A Systematic and Meta-Analytical Review
notes: Systematically reviewed and meta-analyzed the effects of acute sleep loss on multiple physical performance domains including endurance, strength, and power.

title: Sleep extension in athletes: what we know so far - A systematic review
notes: Systematically reviewed evidence on sleep extension interventions (46-113 min beyond habitual ~7 h) and their effects on sport performance in athletes.

title: The effects of sleep extension on the athletic performance of collegiate basketball players
notes: Measured effects of extending sleep to ~10 hours per night on sprint times, shooting accuracy, reaction time, and mood in collegiate basketball players.

title: To Nap or Not to Nap? A Systematic Review Evaluating Napping Behavior in Athletes and the Impact on Various Measures of Athletic Performance
notes: Systematically reviewed napping prevalence, duration, timing, and effects on physical and cognitive performance in athletes, recommending 20-90 min naps between 13:00-16:00.

title: Sleep and Athletic Performance: Impacts on Physical Performance, Mental Performance, Injury Risk and Recovery, and Mental Health
notes: Comprehensively reviewed sleep's multidimensional impact on athletic performance, injury risk, recovery, and mental health across sport populations.

title: General health complaints and sleep associated with new injury within an endurance sporting population: A prospective study
journal: Journal of Science and Medicine in Sport
notes: Prospectively tracked 95 endurance athletes over 52 weeks to examine associations between sleep quantity, subjective health complaints, and new injury occurrence.

title: Sleep Hygiene for Optimizing Recovery in Athletes: Review and Recommendations
notes: Reviewed sleep hygiene strategies and their evidence base for optimizing recovery in athletes, providing practical recommendations for sleep environment, habits, and routines.

title: Partial sleep deprivation affects endurance performance and psychophysiological responses during 12-minute self-paced running exercise
notes: Randomized crossover trial measuring the effect of partial sleep deprivation (beginning vs. end of night) on 12-minute self-paced running distance and psychophysiological responses.

title: Heavy Resistance Training Versus Plyometric Training for Improving Running Economy and Running Time Trial Performance: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
notes: Directly compared heavy resistance training vs plyometric training as supplements to running, finding heavy RT may be superior to plyometrics for improving running economy and time trial performance.

title: Effect of Strength Training on Biomechanical and Neuromuscular Variables in Distance Runners: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
notes: Systematically reviewed and meta-analyzed 25 studies on how concurrent strength and endurance training changes biomechanical and neuromuscular variables in distance runners.

title: Short-term plyometric training improves running economy in highly trained middle and long distance runners
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: RCT examining 9 weeks of plyometric training (3x30 min/week) added to regular training in highly trained runners, demonstrating improved running economy without changes in VO2max.

title: Strength Training Improves Running Economy Durability and Fatigued High-Intensity Performance in Well-Trained Male Runners: A Randomized Control Trial
journal: Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise
notes: RCT showing 10 weeks of twice-weekly maximal strength and plyometric training improved running economy durability and high-intensity time to exhaustion at the end of a 90-minute run.

title: Do Exercise-Based Prevention Programs Reduce Injury in Endurance Runners? A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
notes: Meta-analyzed 9 studies on exercise-based injury prevention in endurance runners, finding no overall significant injury reduction but supervised interventions specifically showed significantly lower injury risk.

title: The Effect of Acute Caffeine Ingestion on Endurance Performance: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
notes: Meta-analysis of 46 studies examining acute caffeine ingestion (3-6 mg/kg) on endurance performance across multiple exercise modalities.

title: International Society of Sports Nutrition Position Stand: Caffeine and Exercise Performance
journal: Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition
notes: ISSN position stand providing comprehensive evidence-based analysis of caffeine supplementation effects on exercise performance across sport modalities.

title: Ergogenic Effect of Nitrate Supplementation: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
notes: Large meta-analysis of 80 studies (111 datasets) examining nitrate supplementation effects on exercise performance across modalities.

title: No Differences Between Beetroot Juice and Placebo on Competitive 5-km Running Performance: A Double-Blind, Placebo-Controlled Trial
journal: International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism
notes: Double-blind RCT examining acute beetroot juice supplementation on competitive 5-km time trial performance in 70 recreational runners.

title: Effects of Vitamin D Supplementation in Elite Athletes: A Systematic Review
notes: Systematic review of 14 RCTs examining vitamin D supplementation effects on aerobic endurance, anaerobic power, and strength in 482 elite athletes.

title: Prevalence of vitamin D inadequacy in athletes: a systematic-review and meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analysis of 23 studies quantifying vitamin D inadequacy prevalence across 2,313 athletes by sport type, geography, and season.

title: β-alanine supplementation to improve exercise capacity and performance: a systematic review and meta-analysis
notes: Large meta-analysis of 40 studies (1,461 participants) examining beta-alanine effects on exercise capacity and performance across various exercise durations.

title: Effects of β-alanine supplementation on exercise performance: a meta-analysis
notes: Early meta-analysis of 15 studies (360 participants) establishing beta-alanine's ergogenic effect for exercise lasting 60-240 seconds.

title: Effects of sodium bicarbonate supplementation on exercise performance: an umbrella review
journal: Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition
notes: Umbrella review of 8 meta-analyses evaluating sodium bicarbonate supplementation effects across exercise types including high-intensity running.

title: Effects of Creatine Monohydrate on Endurance Performance in a Trained Population: A Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
notes: Meta-analysis of 13 studies examining creatine monohydrate supplementation effects on endurance performance in trained athletes.

title: The effects of taper on performance in distance runners
journal: Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise
notes: RCT showing 7-day run taper with 85% volume reduction and maintained high-intensity intervals improved 5 km time by 3% and running economy.

title: Effect of tapering after a period of high-volume sprint interval training on running performance and muscular adaptations in moderately trained runners
notes: Examined 18-day taper following 40 days of high-volume sprint interval training in moderately trained runners, finding improved short-duration running capacity and increased muscle Na+/K+ ATPase expression.

title: Reduced training maintains performance in distance runners
notes: Showed 70% reduction in training volume over 3 weeks maintained 5 km race performance, VO2max, and muscular power, with time to exhaustion increasing 9.5% by week 3.

title: Physiological and performance responses to a 6-day taper in middle-distance runners: influence of training frequency
notes: Compared daily training vs resting every 3rd day during a 6-day taper in middle-distance runners, finding that maintaining daily frequency produced significant 800m performance gains while reduced frequency did not.

title: Tapering strategies in elite British endurance runners
notes: Documented pre-competition tapering practices of elite British endurance runners across middle-distance, long-distance, and marathon events, characterizing taper duration, volume reduction, and intensity patterns by event distance.

title: Scientific bases for precompetition tapering strategies
journal: Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise
notes: Landmark review defining taper as progressive nonlinear training load reduction, recommending 60-90% volume reduction, no more than 20% frequency reduction, maintained intensity, 4-28 day duration, yielding ~3% performance improvement.

title: Physiological changes associated with the pre-event taper in athletes
notes: Comprehensive review cataloguing physiological changes during taper including increased muscle glycogen, hormonal shifts, improved muscle contractile properties, enhanced oxidative enzyme activity, and reduced mood disturbance.

title: Human tendon adaptation in response to mechanical loading: a systematic review and meta-analysis of exercise intervention studies on healthy adults
notes: Systematic review and meta-analysis of 27 studies examining in vivo tendon stiffness, Young's modulus, and cross-sectional area adaptations to chronic mechanical loading in healthy adults.

title: Achilles and patellar tendinopathy loading programmes: a systematic review comparing clinical outcomes and identifying potential mechanisms for effectiveness
notes: Systematic review of 32 studies comparing clinical outcomes of different loading programmes for Achilles and patellar tendinopathy and identifying potential mechanisms of effectiveness.

title: Load progression criteria in exercise programmes in lower limb tendinopathy: a systematic review
notes: Systematic review examining what load progression criteria are used in exercise programmes for lower limb tendinopathy and their evidence base.

title: Heavy-load eccentric calf muscle training for the treatment of chronic Achilles tendinosis
notes: Foundational study introducing the eccentric heel-drop protocol (180 reps/day for 12 weeks) for chronic midportion Achilles tendinosis in recreational athletes.

title: Corticosteroid injections, eccentric decline squat training and heavy slow resistance training in patellar tendinopathy
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine and Science in Sports
notes: Three-arm RCT comparing corticosteroid injections, eccentric decline squats, and heavy slow resistance training over 12 weeks in male patients with patellar tendinopathy.

title: From mechanical loading to collagen synthesis, structural changes and function in human tendon
journal: Scandinavian Journal of Medicine and Science in Sports
notes: Narrative review covering the pathway from mechanical loading to collagen synthesis, including growth factor signaling, gender differences, and structural adaptation in human tendon.

title: The pathogenesis of tendinopathy: balancing the response to loading
notes: Review examining how imbalance between tendon matrix degradation and synthesis in response to mechanical loading contributes to tendinopathy pathogenesis.

title: Effectiveness of the Alfredson protocol compared with a lower repetition-volume protocol for midportion Achilles tendinopathy: a randomized controlled trial
journal: Journal of Orthopaedic and Sports Physical Therapy
notes: RCT comparing the standard Alfredson 180-rep/day eccentric protocol with a lower do-as-tolerated repetition volume protocol over 6 weeks for midportion Achilles tendinopathy.

title: No Difference in Clinical Effects When Comparing Alfredson Eccentric and Silbernagel Combined Concentric-Eccentric Loading in Achilles Tendinopathy: A Randomized Controlled Trial
notes: Multicenter RCT comparing Alfredson isolated eccentric versus Silbernagel combined concentric-eccentric loading programmes over 1 year in recreational athletes with midportion Achilles tendinopathy.

title: Exercise Progression to Incrementally Load the Achilles Tendon
journal: Medicine and Science in Sports and Exercise
notes: Biomechanical study quantifying Achilles tendon loading across a progression of rehabilitation exercises from seated heel raises to single-leg hops, showing a 12-fold range in peak tendon loads.

title: Applied physiology of marathon running
notes: Early comprehensive review of marathon physiology including the role of lactate threshold and OBLA in performance.

title: Modeling: optimal marathon performance on the basis of physiological factors
notes: Modeled marathon performance from VO2max, lactate threshold, and running economy showing a sub-2hr marathon is physiologically possible.

title: The validity of the lactate minimum test for determination of the maximal lactate steady state
notes: Validated the lactate minimum test against MLSS in 13 trained runners and examined physiological predictors of 8km performance.

title: The concept of maximal lactate steady state: a bridge between biochemistry, physiology and sport science
notes: Comprehensive review bridging biochemistry, physiology, and sport science perspectives on the MLSS concept.

title: Maximal lactate steady state as a training stimulus
notes: Examined continuous and intermittent running at MLSS velocity as a training stimulus in 14 moderately trained runners.

title: Critical power: implications for determination of VO2max and exercise tolerance
notes: Reviewed the critical power concept as a fatigue threshold with implications for VO2max determination and exercise tolerance.

title: Blood lactate diagnostics in exercise testing and training
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Reviewed blood lactate diagnostic methods including various threshold concepts and their application to exercise testing and training prescription.

title: Critical Power: An Important Fatigue Threshold in Exercise Physiology
notes: Updated review of the critical power concept as a fundamental fatigue threshold demarcating heavy from severe exercise domains.

title: Does Lactate-Guided Threshold Interval Training within a High-Volume Low-Intensity Approach Represent the "Next Step" in the Evolution of Distance Running Training?
journal: International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health
notes: Proposed a lactate-guided threshold interval training model within high-volume low-intensity context for distance running evolution.

title: The training-injury prevention paradox: should athletes be training smarter and harder?
notes: Proposed the training-injury prevention paradox and recommended maintaining ACWR within 0.8-1.3 across multiple sports.

title: Spikes in acute workload are associated with increased injury risk in elite cricket fast bowlers
notes: Foundational ACWR paper tracking 28 elite cricket fast bowlers over 6 years to show acute workload spikes increase injury risk.

title: The acute:chronic workload ratio predicts injury: high chronic workload may decrease injury risk in elite rugby league players
notes: Demonstrated that ACWR predicts injury in rugby league and that high chronic workloads are protective.

title: Low chronic workload and the acute:chronic workload ratio are more predictive of injury than between-match recovery time: a two-season prospective cohort study in elite rugby league players
notes: Prospective cohort of 28 rugby league players over two seasons comparing ACWR vs recovery time as injury predictors.

title: How do training and competition workloads relate to injury? The workload-injury aetiology model
notes: Proposed an updated injury aetiology model incorporating workloads through exposure, fatigue, and fitness mechanisms.

title: Acute:Chronic Workload Ratio: Conceptual Issues and Fundamental Pitfalls
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Critically examined ACWR's statistical properties and argued there is no causal evidence supporting its use for injury prevention.

title: Acute to chronic workload ratio (ACWR) for predicting sports injury risk: a systematic review and meta-analysis
journal: BMC Sports Science, Medicine and Rehabilitation
notes: Meta-analysis of 22 cohort studies evaluating ACWR effectiveness in predicting sports injuries using Newcastle-Ottawa Scale quality assessment.

title: Increase in the Acute:Chronic Workload Ratio relates to Injury Risk in Competitive Runners
notes: Prospective study tracking 23 competitive runners over 24 months to examine ACWR-injury relationship in running specifically.

title: Comparison of Weekly Training Load and Acute: Chronic Workload Ratio Methods to Estimate Change in Training Load in Running
notes: Compared weekly training load vs ACWR methods for estimating training load changes in 430 runners using 22,839 GPS-tracked sessions.

title: The Association Between Changes in Weekly Running Distance and Running-Related Injury: Preparing for a Half Marathon
journal: Journal of Orthopaedic & Sports Physical Therapy
notes: Prospective cohort of 261 half-marathon runners examining weekly distance changes and running-related injury over 14 weeks.

title: How much is too much? (Part 1) International Olympic Committee consensus statement on load in sport and risk of injury
notes: IOC consensus statement reviewing evidence on training and competition load as risk factors for injury across all sports.

title: Monitoring Athlete Training Loads: Consensus Statement
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Consensus statement from 2016 Doha conference providing a shared framework for training load monitoring concepts and methods.

title: From heart-rate data to training quantification: a comparison of 3 methods of training-intensity analysis
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Compared three HR-based training intensity quantification methods (time-in-zone, session goal, hybrid) across 570 sessions in elite cross-country skiers using 3-zone and 5-zone models.

title: Zone 2 Intensity: A Critical Comparison of Individual Variability in Different Submaximal Exercise Intensity Boundaries
notes: Compared individual variability in Zone 2 boundaries across different physiological markers (VT1, FatMax, %HRmax, blood lactate) in 50 cyclists, finding coefficients of variation from 6-29%.

title: What Is "Zone 2 Training"?: Experts' Viewpoint on Definition, Training Methods, and Expected Adaptations
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Expert consensus statement from 14 sport scientists defining Zone 2 training as intensities below the first lactate/ventilatory threshold, addressing inconsistencies in how the term is used across coaching and research.

title: Contextualizing the Norwegian standardized intensity zone framework in an international sample of endurance practitioners
notes: Survey of 778 international endurance practitioners comparing zone scale preferences (3/5/7-zone) and HR-based zone demarcations across regions and sports, finding Norwegians 2.7x more likely to use a 5-zone scale.

title: Recent advances in training intensity distribution theory for cyclic endurance sports: theoretical foundations, model comparisons, and periodization characteristics
notes: Narrative review comparing theoretical foundations and periodization characteristics of polarized, pyramidal, threshold, and block training intensity distribution models in cyclic endurance sports.

title: State of the Science on Ultramarathon Running After a Half Century: A Systematic Analysis and Commentary
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Systematic analysis of 616 PubMed-indexed ultramarathon publications from 1970-2019, mapping the state of the research field.

title: What are the Limiting Factors During an Ultra-Marathon? A Systematic Review of the Scientific Literature
notes: Systematic review of 23 studies examining performance-limiting factors in ultra-marathons and mountain ultra-marathons (42.2-101 km).

title: Limits of Ultra: Towards an Interdisciplinary Understanding of Ultra-Endurance Running Performance
notes: Interdisciplinary review of physiological, biomechanical, thermoregulatory, gastrointestinal, and psychological factors limiting ultra-endurance running performance.

title: Can neuromuscular fatigue explain running strategies and performance in ultra-marathons?: the flush model
notes: Proposes the 'flush model' of central regulation to explain neuromuscular fatigue, pacing strategies, and performance in ultra-marathon running.

title: Physiological Determinants of Ultramarathon Trail-Running Performance
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Examined how classical physiological fitness measures (VO2max, running economy, body composition) predict performance across 50-km, 80-km, and 160-km trail ultramarathons.

title: Physiology and Pathophysiology in Ultra-Marathon Running
notes: Comprehensive narrative review of ultra-marathon participant characteristics, performance predictors, metabolic demands, and pathophysiological changes during racing.

title: International Society of Sports Nutrition Position Stand: nutritional considerations for single-stage ultra-marathon training and racing
journal: Journal of the International Society of Sports Nutrition
notes: ISSN position stand providing evidence-based nutritional guidelines for single-stage ultra-marathon training and racing, covering macronutrients, hydration, and supplements.

title: Nutrition for Ultramarathon Running: Trail, Track, and Road
journal: International Journal of Sport Nutrition and Exercise Metabolism
notes: Review of nutritional demands and strategies for ultramarathon running across trail, track, and road formats, covering carbohydrate needs, GI issues, and hydration.

title: Is There Evidence for the Development of Sex-Specific Guidelines for Ultramarathon Coaches and Athletes? A Systematic Review
notes: Systematic review of 45 studies comparing male and female ultramarathon runners across physiology, performance, nutrition, and injury to assess evidence for sex-specific guidelines.

title: Epidemiology of Injury and Illness Among Trail Runners: A Systematic Review
notes: Systematic review of 16 studies (8,644 participants) examining injury and illness incidence, prevalence, and risk factors among trail runners.

title: Potential Long-Term Health Problems Associated with Ultra-Endurance Running: A Narrative Review
notes: Narrative review examining potential long-term cardiovascular, renal, musculoskeletal, and gastrointestinal health consequences of ultra-endurance running.

title: The interactions of intensity, frequency and duration of exercise training in altering cardiorespiratory fitness
notes: Reviewed how exercise intensity, frequency, and duration interact to alter cardiorespiratory fitness, finding greatest VO2max gains at 90-100% VO2max intensity.

title: Maximal oxygen uptake: "old" and "new" arguments for a cardiovascular limitation
notes: Presented classical and contemporary arguments that cardiovascular oxygen delivery is the primary limitation for VO2max during whole-body exercise.

title: Determinants of maximal oxygen transport and utilization
notes: Modeled the four critical steps in the O2 transport pathway showing how VO2max depends on ventilation, pulmonary diffusion, circulation, and muscle diffusion.

title: Limiting factors for maximum oxygen uptake and determinants of endurance performance
notes: Reviewed evidence that O2 delivery (not skeletal muscle extraction) is the primary limiter of VO2max, supported by blood doping, hypoxia, and beta-blockade studies.

title: Rate and mechanism of maximal oxygen consumption decline with aging: implications for exercise training
notes: Reviewed rate and mechanisms of VO2max decline with aging, finding approximately 10% per decade decline regardless of activity level.

title: VO2max: what do we know, and what do we still need to know?
notes: Reviewed VO2max as a true parametric measure bounded by the Fick equation, emphasizing enlarged compliant cardiac chambers and greater stroke volume in elite athletes.

title: Endurance exercise performance: the physiology of champions
notes: Reviewed how VO2max, lactate threshold, and efficiency interact to determine endurance performance, with VO2max and LT determining 'performance VO2'.

title: Biology of VO2max: looking under the physiology lamp
notes: Reviewed how red blood cell volume expansion and stroke volume improvements drive training-induced VO2max gains, emphasizing haematological adaptations.

title: The Effect of Exercise Training Intensity on VO2max in Healthy Adults: An Overview of Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses
notes: Umbrella review of systematic reviews and meta-analyses examining the effect of exercise training intensity on VO2max improvements in healthy adults.

title: Effects of warming-up on physical performance: a systematic review with meta-analysis
journal: Journal of Strength and Conditioning Research
notes: Landmark systematic review and meta-analysis of 32 high-quality studies covering 1966-2008. Found that warm-up improved performance in 79% of criteria examined. Provides the strongest broad evidence that warming up before exercise is beneficial across multiple performance domains. Essential reference for justifying warm-up recommendations to runners.

title: Do We Need a Cool-Down After Exercise? A Narrative Review of the Psychophysiological Effects and the Effects on Performance, Injuries and the Long-Term Adaptive Response
notes: The definitive review challenging conventional cool-down wisdom. Concluded that active cool-downs are largely ineffective for enhancing same-day or next-day performance, do not prevent injuries, do not reduce muscle soreness, and do not attenuate long-term adaptation. Active cool-downs do accelerate blood lactate clearance but not muscle lactate. Critical paper for a running coach AI to avoid over-prescribing unnecessary cool-down routines.

title: Acute Effects of Dynamic Stretching on Muscle Flexibility and Performance: An Analysis of the Current Literature
notes: Comprehensive systematic review of dynamic stretching effects on flexibility and performance. Found substantial evidence that dynamic stretching improves ROM and subsequent performance (force, power, sprint, jump). Explains mechanisms: ROM gains from reduced muscle-tendon unit stiffness, performance gains from temperature and potentiation-related mechanisms. Provides the physiological rationale for recommending dynamic over static stretching in pre-run warm-ups.

title: Dynamic Warm-ups Play Pivotal Role in Athletic Performance and Injury Prevention
notes: Recent clinical review synthesizing evidence on dynamic warm-ups for performance and injury prevention. Recommends 7-10 minute dynamic warm-up including agility, plyometrics, power, core, and balance activities. Highlights that static stretching temporarily reduces muscle strength by up to 5.4% while dynamic warm-ups prime muscles without that cost. Directly applicable for designing pre-run warm-up protocols in a coaching AI.

title: Moderate-Duration Dynamic Stretching During Warm-up Improves Running Economy and Running Performance in Recreational Distance Runners
journal: International Journal of Sports Physiology and Performance
notes: Running-specific RCT comparing moderate-duration dynamic stretching vs static stretching vs no stretching during warm-up in recreational distance runners. Dynamic stretching significantly improved running economy and enhanced total running distance and time to exhaustion, while 60 seconds of static stretching did not produce significant improvements. Directly supports prescribing dynamic stretching in pre-run warm-ups for distance runners.

title: Post-activation potentiation (PAP) in endurance sports: A review
notes: Reviewed 22 studies examining post-activation potentiation (PAP) in endurance athletes, discussing physiological mechanisms, optimal conditioning activity parameters, and the potential for PAP to enhance endurance performance when incorporated into warm-up routines.

title: No Evidence of Postactivation Performance Enhancement on Endurance Exercises: A Comprehensive Systematic Review and Meta-analysis
